title: International Phonetic Alphabet

infobox.value: Used for [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") and [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") transcription of any oral language

[Egyptian hieroglyphs](/wiki/Egyptian_hieroglyphs "Egyptian hieroglyphs")  * [Proto-Sinaitic script](/wiki/Proto-Sinaitic_script "Proto-Sinaitic script")   + [Phoenician alphabet](/wiki/Phoenician_alphabet "Phoenician alphabet")     - [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet")       * [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script")         + [Lepsius Standard Alphabet](/wiki/Lepsius_Standard_Alphabet "Lepsius Standard Alphabet")           - [Palaeotype alphabet](/wiki/Palaeotype_alphabet "Palaeotype alphabet"), [English Phonotypic Alphabet](/wiki/English_Phonotypic_Alphabet "English Phonotypic Alphabet")             * [Romic alphabet](/wiki/Romic_alphabet "Romic alphabet")               + International Phonetic Alphabet
*See [Phonetic symbols in Unicode § Unicode blocks](/wiki/Phonetic_symbols_in_Unicode#Unicode_blocks "Phonetic symbols in Unicode")*

Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Special_characters "Help:Special characters"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of phonetic symbols.

(**IPA**) is an [alphabetic](/wiki/Alphabet "Alphabet") system of [phonetic notation](/wiki/Phonetic_notation "Phonetic notation") based primarily on the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"). It was devised by the [International Phonetic Association](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Association "International Phonetic Association") in the late 19th century as a standard written representation for the sounds of [speech](/wiki/Speech "Speech").[[1]](#cite_note-IPA_1999-1) The IPA is used by [linguists](/wiki/Linguists "Linguists"), [lexicographers](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography"), foreign language students and teachers, [speech–language pathologists](/wiki/Speech%E2%80%93language_pathology "Speech–language pathology"), singers, actors, [constructed language](/wiki/Constructed_language "Constructed language") creators, and [translators](/wiki/Translators "Translators").[[2]](#cite_note-world-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

System of phonetic notation

For the spelling alphabet used in spoken communication over radio, see [NATO phonetic alphabet](/wiki/NATO_phonetic_alphabet "NATO phonetic alphabet").

For an introductory guide on IPA symbols with audio, see [Help:IPA](/wiki/Help:IPA "Help:IPA"). For the usage of the IPA on Wikipedia, see [Help:IPA/Introduction](/wiki/Help:IPA/Introduction "Help:IPA/Introduction") and [Help:IPA/English](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English").

**This article contains [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") [symbols](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Special_characters "Help:Special characters"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)") instead of phonetic symbols.

The **International Phonetic Alphabet** (**IPA**) is an [alphabetic](/wiki/Alphabet "Alphabet") system of [phonetic notation](/wiki/Phonetic_notation "Phonetic notation") based primarily on the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"). It was devised by the [International Phonetic Association](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Association "International Phonetic Association") in the late 19th century as a standard written representation for the sounds of [speech](/wiki/Speech "Speech").[[1]](#cite_note-IPA_1999-1) The IPA is used by [linguists](/wiki/Linguists "Linguists"), [lexicographers](/wiki/Lexicography "Lexicography"), foreign language students and teachers, [speech–language pathologists](/wiki/Speech%E2%80%93language_pathology "Speech–language pathology"), singers, actors, [constructed language](/wiki/Constructed_language "Constructed language") creators, and [translators](/wiki/Translators "Translators").[[2]](#cite_note-world-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The IPA is designed to represent those qualities of [speech](/wiki/Speech "Speech") that are part of [lexical](/wiki/Lexical_item "Lexical item") (and, to a limited extent, [prosodic](/wiki/Prosodic "Prosodic")) sounds in [spoken (oral) language](/wiki/Oral_language "Oral language"): [phones](/wiki/Phone_(phonetics) "Phone (phonetics)"), [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(linguistics) "Intonation (linguistics)") and the separation of [syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable").[[1]](#cite_note-IPA_1999-1) To represent additional qualities of speech – such as tooth [gnashing](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/gnash "wikt:gnash"), [lisping](/wiki/Lisp "Lisp"), and sounds made with a [cleft palate](/wiki/Cleft_lip_and_cleft_palate "Cleft lip and cleft palate") – an [extended set of symbols](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet") may be used.[[2]](#cite_note-world-2)

Segments are [transcribed](/wiki/Transcription_(linguistics) "Transcription (linguistics)") by one or more IPA symbols of two basic types: [letters](/wiki/Letter_(alphabet) "Letter (alphabet)") and [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic"). For example, the sound of the English letter ⟨t⟩ may be transcribed in IPA with a single letter: [t], or with a letter plus diacritics: [t̺ʰ], depending on how precise one wishes to be. Similarly, the French letter ⟨t⟩ may be transcribed as either [t] or [t̻]: [t̺ʰ] and [t̻] are two different, though similar, sounds. Slashes are used to signal [phonemic transcription](/wiki/Phonemic_transcription "Phonemic transcription"); therefore, /t/ is more abstract than either [t̺ʰ] or [t̻] and might refer to either, depending on the context and language.[[note 1]](#cite_note-4)

Occasionally, letters or diacritics are added, removed, or modified by the International Phonetic Association. As of the most recent change in 2005,[[4]](#cite_note-5) there are 107 segmental letters, an indefinitely large number of suprasegmental letters, 44 diacritics (not counting composites), and four extra-lexical [prosodic](/wiki/Prosodic "Prosodic") marks in the IPA. These are illustrated in the current [IPA chart](/wiki/IPA_chart "IPA chart"), posted below in this article and on the International Phonetic Association's website.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/History_of_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "History of the International Phonetic Alphabet")

In 1886, a group of French and English language teachers, led by the French linguist [Paul Passy](/wiki/Paul_Passy "Paul Passy"), formed what would be known from 1897 onwards as the [International Phonetic Association](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Association "International Phonetic Association") (in French, *l'Association phonétique internationale*).[[6]](#cite_note-IPA194-196-7) The idea of the alphabet had been suggested to Passy by [Otto Jespersen](/wiki/Otto_Jespersen "Otto Jespersen"). It was developed by Passy along with other members of the association, principally [Daniel Jones](/wiki/Daniel_Jones_(phonetician) "Daniel Jones (phonetician)").
The original IPA alphabet was based on the [Romic alphabet](/wiki/Romic_alphabet "Romic alphabet"), an English [spelling reform](/wiki/Spelling_reform "Spelling reform") created by [Henry Sweet](/wiki/Henry_Sweet "Henry Sweet") that, in turn, was based on the [Palaeotype alphabet](/wiki/Palaeotype_alphabet "Palaeotype alphabet") of [Alexander John Ellis](/wiki/Alexander_John_Ellis "Alexander John Ellis"), itself derived from [Lepsius Standard Alphabet](/wiki/Lepsius_Standard_Alphabet "Lepsius Standard Alphabet") first used for transcribing Ancient Egyptian into German.

The original intent was to make it usable for other languages; the values of the symbols were allowed to vary from language to language.[[note 2]](#cite_note-9) For example, the sound [[ʃ](/wiki/Voiceless_postalveolar_fricative "Voiceless postalveolar fricative")] (the *sh* in *shoe*) was originally represented with the letter ⟨c⟩ for English but with ⟨x⟩ for French and German; with German, ⟨c⟩ was used for the [[x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative")] sound of *[Bach](/wiki/Bach "Bach")*.[[6]](#cite_note-IPA194-196-7) With a growing number of transcribed languages this proved impractical, and in 1888 the values of the letters were made uniform across languages. This would provide the base for all future revisions.[[6]](#cite_note-IPA194-196-7)[[8]](#cite_note-10)

Since its creation, the IPA has undergone a number of revisions. After relatively frequent revisions and expansions from the 1890s to the 1940s, the IPA remained nearly static until the [Kiel Convention](/wiki/Kiel_Convention "Kiel Convention") in 1989, which substantially revamped the alphabet. A smaller revision took place in 1993 with the resurrection of letters for [mid central vowels](/wiki/Mid_central_vowel "Mid central vowel")[[2]](#cite_note-world-2) and the retirement of letters for [voiceless implosives](/wiki/Implosive_consonant#Voiceless_implosives "Implosive consonant").[[9]](#cite_note-Pullum-11) The alphabet was last revised in May 2005 with the addition of a letter for a [labiodental flap](/wiki/Labiodental_flap "Labiodental flap").[[10]](#cite_note-12) Apart from the addition and removal of symbols, changes to the IPA have consisted largely of renaming symbols and categories and in modifying [typefaces](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface").[[2]](#cite_note-world-2)

[Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet") for [speech pathology](/wiki/Speech_pathology "Speech pathology") (extIPA) were created in 1990 and were officially adopted by the [International Clinical Phonetics and Linguistics Association](/wiki/International_Clinical_Phonetics_and_Linguistics_Association "International Clinical Phonetics and Linguistics Association") in 1994.[[11]](#cite_note-13)
They were substantially revised in 2015 with lesser changes in 2025.

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Description")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/IPA_chart_2020.svg/500px-IPA_chart_2020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:IPA_chart_2020.svg)

The official summary chart of the IPA, revised in 2020

The general principle of the IPA is to provide one letter for each distinctive sound ([phoneme](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme")).[[note 3]](#cite_note-15) This means that:

* It does not use [combinations of letters](/wiki/Multigraph_(orthography) "Multigraph (orthography)") to represent single sounds, the way English does with ⟨sh⟩ and ⟨ea⟩, nor single letters to represent multiple sounds, the way ⟨x⟩ represents /ks/ or /ɡz/ in English.[[note 4]](#cite_note-16)
* There are no letters that have context-dependent sound values, the way English ⟨[c](/wiki/Hard_and_soft_C "Hard and soft C")⟩ and ⟨[g](/wiki/Hard_and_soft_G "Hard and soft G")⟩ have a "hard" and "soft" pronunciation.
* The IPA does not generally have separate letters for two sounds if no known language makes a distinction between them, a property known as "selectiveness".[[2]](#cite_note-world-2)[[note 5]](#cite_note-17) However, if a large number of phonemically distinct letters can be derived with a single diacritic, that may be used instead.[[note 6]](#cite_note-18)

The alphabet is designed for transcribing sounds (phones), not [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme"), though it is used for phonemic transcription as well. A few letters that did not indicate specific sounds have been retired – ⟨ˇ⟩, once used for the "compound" tone of Swedish and Norwegian, and ⟨ƞ⟩, once used for the [moraic](/wiki/Moraic "Moraic") nasal of Japanese – though one remains: ⟨ɧ⟩, used for the [sj-sound](/wiki/Sj-sound "Sj-sound") of Swedish. When the IPA is used for broad phonetic or for phonemic transcription, the letter–sound correspondence can be rather loose. The IPA has recommended that more 'familiar' letters be used when that would not cause ambiguity.[[13]](#cite_note-principles49-familiar-19) For example, ⟨e⟩ and ⟨o⟩ for [ɛ] and [ɔ], ⟨t⟩ for [t̪] or [ʈ], ⟨f⟩ for [ɸ], etc. Indeed, in the illustration of Hindi in the IPA *Handbook*, the letters ⟨c⟩ and ⟨ɟ⟩ are used for /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/; in the 1949 *Principles*, they had been used as shortcuts for /t͡ɕ/ and /d͡ʑ/ in Twi.

Among the symbols of the IPA, 107 letters represent [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") and [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"), 31 [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") are used to modify these, and 17 additional signs indicate [suprasegmental](/wiki/Segment_(linguistics) "Segment (linguistics)") qualities such as [length](/wiki/Length_(phonetics) "Length (phonetics)"), [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"), [stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)"), and [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(linguistics) "Intonation (linguistics)").[[note 7]](#cite_note-20) These are organized into a chart; the chart displayed here is the official chart as posted at the website of the IPA.

### Letter forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Letter forms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/LowercaseG.svg/250px-LowercaseG.svg.png)](/wiki/File:LowercaseG.svg)

Loop-tail ⟨g⟩ and open-tail ⟨ɡ⟩ are graphic variants. Open-tail ⟨ɡ⟩ was the original IPA symbol, but both are now considered correct. See [history of the IPA](/wiki/History_of_the_IPA "History of the IPA") for details.

The International Phonetic Alphabet is based on the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), and uses as few non-Latin letters as possible.[[6]](#cite_note-IPA194-196-7) The non-Latin letters are meant to harmonize with the Latin letters.[[note 8]](#cite_note-22) For this reason, most letters are either Latin, [Greek](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet"), or modifications thereof. Some letters are neither: for example, the letter denoting the [glottal stop](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop"), ⟨ʔ⟩, originally had the form of a [question mark](/wiki/Question_mark "Question mark") with the dot removed. A few letters, such as that of the [voiced pharyngeal fricative](/wiki/Voiced_pharyngeal_fricative "Voiced pharyngeal fricative"), ⟨ʕ⟩, were inspired by other writing systems (in this case, the [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script") letter ⟨[ﻉ](/wiki/Ayin "Ayin")⟩, **ʿayn**, via the reversed apostrophe).[[9]](#cite_note-Pullum-11)

The Association created the IPA so that the sound values of most letters would correspond to "international usage" (approximately [Classical Latin](/wiki/Classical_Latin "Classical Latin")).[[6]](#cite_note-IPA194-196-7) Hence, the consonant letters ⟨b⟩, ⟨d⟩, ⟨f⟩, ⟨ɡ⟩, ⟨h⟩, ⟨k⟩, ⟨l⟩, ⟨m⟩, ⟨n⟩, ⟨p⟩, ⟨s⟩, ⟨t⟩, ⟨v⟩, ⟨w⟩, and ⟨z⟩ have more or less their word-initial values in English (*g* as in *gill*, *h* as in *hill*, though *p t k* are unaspirated as in *spill, still, skill*); and the vowel letters ⟨a⟩, ⟨e⟩, ⟨i⟩, ⟨o⟩, ⟨u⟩ correspond to the (long) sound values of Latin: [i] is like the vowel in *mach**i**ne*, [u] is as in *r**u**le*, etc. Other Latin letters, particularly ⟨j⟩, ⟨r⟩ and ⟨y⟩, differ from English, but have their IPA values in Latin or other European languages.

Beyond the letters themselves, there are secondary symbols which aid in transcription. [Diacritic](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") marks can be combined with the letters to add tone and phonetic detail such as [secondary articulation](/wiki/Secondary_articulation "Secondary articulation"). There are also special symbols for prosodic features such as stress and intonation.

#### Typography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Typography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Turned_small_cap_omega_as_a_vowel.svg/250px-Turned_small_cap_omega_as_a_vowel.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Turned_small_cap_omega_as_a_vowel.svg)

An example of a font that uses turned small-capital omega for the vowel letter ⟨ʊ⟩. The glyph had originally been a small-capital ⟨ᴜ⟩.

The basic Latin inventory was extended by adding small-capital and cursive forms, overlapping diacritics such as hooks, and rotation. The sound values of these letters are related to those of the original letters or to those of letters that they were modified to resemble.[[note 9]](#cite_note-24)
For example, [rotated letters](/wiki/Rotated_letter "Rotated letter") were popular in the era of [mechanical typesetting](/wiki/Mechanical_typesetting "Mechanical typesetting"), as they had the advantage of not requiring the casting of special type for IPA symbols, much as the sorts for ⟨b⟩ and ⟨q⟩, ⟨d⟩ and ⟨p⟩, ⟨n⟩ and ⟨u⟩, and ⟨6⟩ and ⟨9⟩ had traditionally often pulled double duty to reduce printers' costs.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Thus rotated ⟨ɐ ə ɹ ʍ⟩ recall *a e r w*, while rotated ⟨ɔ ɟ ɓ ɥ ɯ ʌ ʎ⟩ recall *o j b y u/w ᴀ y/λ*.[[note 10]](#cite_note-25)

There are several letters from the Greek alphabet, though their sound values may differ from Greek. For most Greek letters, subtly different [glyph](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") shapes have been devised for the IPA, specifically ⟨ꞵ⟩, ⟨ɣ⟩, ⟨ɛ⟩, ⟨ɸ⟩, ⟨ꭓ⟩ and ⟨ʋ⟩, which are encoded in [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") separately from their parent Greek letters. One, however – ⟨θ⟩ – has only its Greek form, while for ⟨ꞵ ~ β⟩ and ⟨ꭓ ~ χ⟩, both Greek and Latin forms are in common use.[[16]](#cite_note-26)

#### Iconicity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Iconicity")]

The graphic derivation of letters and diacritics may be [iconic](/wiki/Iconicity "Iconicity"):

* A rightward-facing hooked tail, as in ⟨ʈ ɖ ɳ ɽ ʂ ʐ ɻ ɭ ⟩, indicates [retroflex](/wiki/Retroflex "Retroflex") articulation. It originates from the hooked arm of an *r*.
* The top hook, as in ⟨ɠ ɗ ɓ⟩, indicates [implosion](/wiki/Glottalic_ingressive "Glottalic ingressive").
* Several [nasal consonants](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") are based on the form ⟨n⟩: ⟨n ɲ ɳ ŋ⟩. ⟨ɲ⟩ and ⟨ŋ⟩ derive from [ligatures](/wiki/Typographic_ligature "Typographic ligature") of *gn* and *ng*.[[note 11]](#cite_note-27)
* Among consonant letters, the [small capital](/wiki/Small_capital "Small capital") letters ⟨ɢ ʜ ʟ ɴ ʀ ʁ⟩, and also ⟨ꞯ⟩ in [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA"), indicate more [guttural](/wiki/Guttural "Guttural") sounds than their base letters – ⟨ʙ⟩ is a late exception. Among vowel letters, the small capitals ⟨ɪ ʏ ʊ⟩ indicate what had originally been considered more [lax](/wiki/Lax_vowel "Lax vowel") articulations than their base letters; ⟨ʊ⟩ had originally been ⟨ᴜ⟩.[[17]](#cite_note-28) Again, small-cap ⟨ɶ⟩ is a late exception.
* The [tone letters](/wiki/Tone_letter#IPA "Tone letter") derive from a [pitch trace](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") on a [musical scale](/wiki/Musical_scale "Musical scale").

### Other representations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Other representations")]

Main articles: [Cursive forms of the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Cursive_forms_of_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Cursive forms of the International Phonetic Alphabet") and [IPA Braille](/wiki/IPA_Braille "IPA Braille")

IPA letters have [cursive](/wiki/Cursive "Cursive") forms designed for use in manuscripts and when taking field notes, but the *Handbook* recommended against their use, as cursive IPA is "harder for most people to decipher".[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEInternational_Phonetic_Association199931-29) A [braille](/wiki/Braille "Braille") representation of the IPA for blind or visually impaired professionals and students has also been developed.[[19]](#cite_note-30)

## Modifying the IPA chart

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Modifying the IPA chart")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Extended_IPA_chart_2005.png/500px-Extended_IPA_chart_2005.png)](/wiki/File:Extended_IPA_chart_2005.png)

The authors of textbooks or similar publications often create revised versions of the IPA chart to express their own preferences or needs. The image displays one such version. All pulmonic consonants are moved to the consonant chart. Only the blackwhite symbols are on the official IPA chart; additional symbols are in grey. The grey fricatives are part of the [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA"), and the grey retroflex letters are mentioned or implicit in the *Handbook*. The grey click is a retired IPA letter that is still in use.

The International Phonetic Alphabet is occasionally modified by the Association. After each modification, the Association provides an updated simplified presentation of the alphabet in the form of a chart. (See [History of the IPA](/wiki/History_of_the_IPA "History of the IPA").) Not all aspects of the alphabet can be accommodated in a chart of the size published by the IPA. The [alveolo-palatal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal "Alveolo-palatal") and [epiglottal](/wiki/Epiglottal "Epiglottal") consonants, for example, are not included in the consonant chart for reasons of space rather than of theory (two additional columns would be required, one between the retroflex and palatal columns and the other between the pharyngeal and glottal columns), and the [lateral flap](/wiki/Lateral_flap "Lateral flap") would require an additional row for that single consonant, so they are listed instead under the catchall block of "other symbols".[[20]](#cite_note-31) The indefinitely large number of [tone letters](/wiki/Chao_tone_letter "Chao tone letter") would make a full accounting impractical even on a larger page, and only a few examples are shown, and even the tone diacritics are not complete; the reversed tone letters are not illustrated at all.

The procedure for modifying the alphabet or the chart is to propose the change in the *[Journal of the IPA](/wiki/Journal_of_the_IPA "Journal of the IPA").* (See, for example, December 2008 on an [open central unrounded vowel](/wiki/Open_central_unrounded_vowel "Open central unrounded vowel")[[21]](#cite_note-32) and August 2011 on central approximants.)[[22]](#cite_note-cambridge1-33) Reactions to the proposal may be published in the same or subsequent issues of the Journal (as in August 2009 on the open central vowel).[[23]](#cite_note-34)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] A formal proposal is then put to the Council of the IPA[[24]](#cite_note-35)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] – which is elected by the membership[[25]](#cite_note-36) – for further discussion and a formal vote.[[26]](#cite_note-37)[[27]](#cite_note-38)

Many users of the alphabet, including the leadership of the Association itself, deviate from its standardized usage.[[28]](#cite_note-39)
The *Journal of the IPA* finds it acceptable to mix IPA and [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA") symbols in consonant charts in their articles. (For instance, including the extIPA letter ⟨[𝼆](/wiki/Voiceless_palatal_lateral_fricative "Voiceless palatal lateral fricative")⟩, rather than ⟨ʎ̝̊⟩, in an illustration of the IPA.)[[29]](#cite_note-pulmonic-40)

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Usage")]

Further information: [Phonetic transcription](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription")

Of more than 160 IPA symbols, relatively few will be used to transcribe speech in any one language, with various levels of precision. A precise phonetic transcription, in which sounds are specified in detail, is known as a *narrow transcription*. A coarser transcription with less detail is called a *broad transcription.* Both are relative terms, and both are generally enclosed in square brackets.[[1]](#cite_note-IPA_1999-1) Broad phonetic transcriptions may restrict themselves to easily heard details, or only to details that are relevant to the discussion at hand, and may differ little if at all from phonemic transcriptions, but they make no theoretical claim that all the distinctions transcribed are necessarily meaningful in the language. The term 'broad' may furthermore carry implication that diacritics are avoided (at least as far as possible) or even that the transcription is restricted to the letters of the [ISO basic Latin alphabet](/wiki/ISO_basic_Latin_alphabet "ISO basic Latin alphabet").[[30]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/RPGA_international.svg/250px-RPGA_international.svg.png)](/wiki/File:RPGA_international.svg)

Phonetic transcriptions of the word *international* in two English dialects

For example, the English word *little* may be transcribed broadly as [ˈlɪtəl], approximately describing many pronunciations. A narrower transcription may focus on individual or dialectical details: [ˈɫɪɾɫ] in [General American](/wiki/General_American "General American"), [ˈlɪʔo] in [Cockney](/wiki/Cockney "Cockney"), or [ˈɫɪːɫ] in [Southern US English](/wiki/Southern_US_English "Southern US English").

Phonemic transcriptions, which express the conceptual counterparts of spoken sounds, are usually enclosed in slashes (/ /) and tend to use simpler letters with few diacritics. The choice of IPA letters may reflect theoretical claims of how speakers conceptualize sounds as phonemes or they may be merely a convenience for typesetting. Phonemic approximations between slashes do not have absolute sound values. For instance, in English, either the vowel of *pick* or the vowel of *peak* may be transcribed as /i/, so that *pick*, *peak* would be transcribed as /ˈpik, ˈpiːk/ or as /ˈpɪk, ˈpik/; and neither is identical to the vowel of the French **pique**, which would also be transcribed /pik/. By contrast, a narrow phonetic transcription of *pick*, *peak*, *pique* could be: [pʰɪk], [pʰiːk], [pikʲ].

### Linguists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Linguists")]

IPA is popular for transcription by linguists. Some American linguists, however, use a mix of IPA with [Americanist phonetic notation](/wiki/Americanist_phonetic_notation "Americanist phonetic notation") or [Sinological phonetic notation](/wiki/Sinological_phonetic_notation "Sinological phonetic notation") or otherwise use [nonstandard symbols](/wiki/Obsolete_and_nonstandard_symbols_in_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Obsolete and nonstandard symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet") for various reasons.[[31]](#cite_note-thomason-42) Authors who employ such nonstandard use are encouraged to include a chart or other explanation of their choices, which is good practice in general, as linguists differ in their understanding of the exact meaning of IPA symbols and common conventions change over time.

### Dictionaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dictionaries")]

#### English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: English")]

Many British dictionaries, including the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* and some [learner's dictionaries](/wiki/Monolingual_learner%27s_dictionary "Monolingual learner's dictionary") such as the *[Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_Advanced_Learner%27s_Dictionary "Oxford Advanced Learner's Dictionary")* and the *[Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary](/wiki/Cambridge_Advanced_Learner%27s_Dictionary "Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary")*, now use [the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Pronunciation_respelling_for_English#International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Pronunciation respelling for English") to represent the pronunciation of words.[[32]](#cite_note-43) However, most American (and some British) volumes use one of a variety of [pronunciation respelling](/wiki/Pronunciation_respelling_for_English "Pronunciation respelling for English") systems, intended to be more comfortable for readers of English and to be more acceptable across dialects, without the implication of a preferred pronunciation that the IPA might convey. For example, the respelling systems in many American dictionaries (such as *[Merriam-Webster](/wiki/Merriam-Webster_Dictionary "Merriam-Webster Dictionary")*) use ⟨y⟩ for IPA [ j] and ⟨sh⟩ for IPA [ ʃ ], reflecting the usual spelling of those sounds in English.[[33]](#cite_note-44)[[34]](#cite_note-45)[[note 12]](#cite_note-46)
(In IPA, [y] represents the sound of the French ⟨u⟩, as in **tu**, and [sh] represents the sequence of consonants in *gra**ssh**opper*.)

#### Other languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Other languages")]

The IPA is also not universal among dictionaries in languages other than English. Monolingual dictionaries of languages with [phonemic orthographies](/wiki/Phonemic_orthographies "Phonemic orthographies") generally do not bother with indicating the pronunciation of most words, and tend to use respelling systems for words with unexpected pronunciations. Dictionaries produced in Israel use the IPA rarely and sometimes use the [Hebrew alphabet](/wiki/Hebrew_alphabet "Hebrew alphabet") for transcription of foreign words.[[note 13]](#cite_note-47) Bilingual dictionaries that translate from foreign languages into Russian usually employ the IPA, but monolingual Russian dictionaries occasionally use pronunciation respelling for foreign words.[[note 14]](#cite_note-48) The IPA is more common in bilingual dictionaries, but there are exceptions here too. Mass-market bilingual Czech dictionaries, for instance, tend to use the IPA only for sounds not found in [Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language").[[note 15]](#cite_note-50)

### Standard orthographies and case variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Standard orthographies and case variants")]

Main article: [Case variants of IPA letters](/wiki/Case_variants_of_IPA_letters "Case variants of IPA letters")

The IPA alphabet does not have casing. Since 1907, proper nouns (primarily place names) had been marked by a prefixed asterisk ⟨\*⟩ in the IPA journal *[Le Maître Phonétique](/wiki/Le_Ma%C3%AEtre_Phon%C3%A9tique "Le Maître Phonétique")*, which was written in IPA transcription rather than in English or French orthography. This practice would continue through the final publication in 1970. The 1949 edition of the IPA handbook made it explicit,[[36]](#cite_note-51) though it was not included in the 1999 *Handbook*, which notes the contrary use of the asterisk as a placeholder for a sound or feature that does not have a symbol, one of the extIPA conventions that had been agreed upon at the 1989 Kiel Convention.[[37]](#cite_note-52)

However, casing is required when IPA letters are incorporated into orthographies. Such orthographies are used in many sub-Saharan languages such as [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language"), [Fula](/wiki/Fula_language "Fula language"), [Akan](/wiki/Akan_language "Akan language"), [Gbe languages](/wiki/Gbe_languages "Gbe languages"), [Manding languages](/wiki/Manding_languages "Manding languages"), [Lingala](/wiki/Lingala "Lingala"), etc., often via the [Africa Alphabet](/wiki/Africa_Alphabet "Africa Alphabet"). Capital case variants have been created for use in these languages. For example, [Kabiyè](/wiki/Kabiy%C3%A9_language "Kabiyé language") of northern [Togo](/wiki/Togo "Togo") has [Ɖ ɖ](/wiki/African_D "African D"), [Ŋ ŋ](/wiki/Eng_(letter) "Eng (letter)"), [Ɣ ɣ](/wiki/Latin_gamma "Latin gamma"), [Ɔ ɔ](/wiki/Open_O "Open O"), [Ɛ ɛ](/wiki/Latin_epsilon "Latin epsilon") and [Ʋ ʋ](/wiki/%C6%B2 "Ʋ"). These, and others, are supported by [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode"), but appear in Latin ranges other than the [IPA extensions](/wiki/IPA_Extensions_(Unicode_block) "IPA Extensions (Unicode block)").

### Classical singing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Classical singing")]

The IPA has widespread use among classical singers during preparation as they are frequently required to sing in a variety of foreign languages. They are also taught by vocal coaches to perfect diction and improve tone quality and tuning.[[38]](#cite_note-53) Opera [librettos](/wiki/Libretto "Libretto") are authoritatively transcribed in IPA, such as [Nico Castel](/wiki/Nico_Castel "Nico Castel")'s volumes[[39]](#cite_note-54) and Timothy Cheek's book *Singing in Czech*.[[40]](#cite_note-55) Opera singers' ability to read IPA was used by the site *Visual Thesaurus*, which employed several opera singers "to make recordings for the 150,000 words and phrases in VT's lexical database ... for their vocal stamina, attention to the details of enunciation, and most of all, knowledge of IPA".[[41]](#cite_note-56)

## Letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Letters")]

See also: [International Phonetic Alphabet chart](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet_chart "International Phonetic Alphabet chart")

The International Phonetic Association organizes the letters of the IPA into three categories: [pulmonic](/wiki/Pulmonic "Pulmonic") consonants, non-pulmonic consonants, and vowels.[[note 16]](#cite_note-58)[[43]](#cite_note-59)

Pulmonic consonant letters are arranged singly or in pairs of voiceless ([tenuis](/wiki/Tenuis_consonant "Tenuis consonant")) and voiced sounds, with these then grouped in columns from front (labial) sounds on the left to back (glottal) sounds on the right. In official publications by the IPA, two columns are omitted to save space, with the letters listed among "other symbols" even though theoretically they belong in the main chart.[[note 17]](#cite_note-61) They are arranged in rows from full closure (occlusives: stops and nasals) at top, to brief closure (vibrants: trills and taps), to partial closure (fricatives), and finally minimal closure (approximants) at bottom, again with a row left out to save space. In the table below, a slightly different arrangement is made: All pulmonic consonants are included in the pulmonic-consonant table, and the vibrants and laterals are separated out so that the rows reflect the common [lenition](/wiki/Lenition "Lenition") pathway of *stop → fricative → approximant*, as well as the fact that several letters pull double duty as both fricative and approximant; [affricates](/wiki/Affricate "Affricate") may then be created by joining stops and fricatives from adjacent cells. Shaded cells represent articulations that are judged to be impossible or not distinctive.

Vowel letters are also grouped in pairs – of unrounded and rounded vowel sounds – with these pairs also arranged from front on the left to back on the right, and from maximal closure at top to minimal closure at bottom. No vowel letters are omitted from the chart, though in the past some of the mid central vowels were listed among the "other symbols".

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Consonants")]

See also: [IPA consonant chart with audio](/wiki/IPA_consonant_chart_with_audio "IPA consonant chart with audio")

#### Pulmonic consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Pulmonic consonants")]

A [pulmonic](/wiki/Egressive "Egressive") consonant is a consonant made by obstructing the [glottis](/wiki/Glottis "Glottis") (the space between the vocal folds) or [oral cavity](/wiki/Human_mouth "Human mouth") (the mouth) and either simultaneously or subsequently letting out air from the lungs. Pulmonic consonants make up the majority of consonants in the IPA, as well as in human language. All consonants in English fall into this category.[[45]](#cite_note-62)

The pulmonic consonant table, which includes most consonants, is arranged in rows that designate [manner of articulation](/wiki/Manner_of_articulation "Manner of articulation"), meaning how the consonant is produced, and columns that designate [place of articulation](/wiki/Place_of_articulation "Place of articulation"), meaning where in the vocal tract the consonant is produced. The main chart includes only consonants with a single place of articulation.

**Notes**

* In rows where some letters appear in pairs (the *[obstruents](/wiki/Obstruent "Obstruent")*), the letter to the right represents a [voiced consonant](/wiki/Voiced_consonant "Voiced consonant"), except [breathy-voiced](/wiki/Breathy-voiced "Breathy-voiced") [ɦ].[[46]](#cite_note-63) In the other rows (the *[sonorants](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant")*), the single letter represents a voiced consonant.
* While IPA provides a single letter for the coronal places of articulation (for all consonants but fricatives), these do not always have to be used exactly. When dealing with a particular language, the letters may be treated as specifically dental, alveolar, or post-alveolar, as appropriate for that language, without diacritics.
* Shaded areas indicate articulations judged to be impossible.
* The letters [β, ð, ʁ, ʕ, ʢ] are canonically voiced fricatives but may be used for approximants.[[note 18]](#cite_note-65)
* In many languages, such as English or Korean, [h] and [ɦ] are not actually glottal, fricatives, or approximants. Rather, they are bare [phonation](/wiki/Phonation "Phonation").[[48]](#cite_note-66)
* It is primarily the shape of the tongue rather than its position that distinguishes the fricatives [ʃ ʒ], [ɕ ʑ], and [ʂ ʐ].
* [ʜ, ʢ] are defined as epiglottal fricatives under the "Other symbols" section in the official IPA chart, but they may be treated as trills at the same place of articulation as [ħ, ʕ] because trilling of the [aryepiglottic folds](/wiki/Aryepiglottic_fold "Aryepiglottic fold") typically co-occurs.[[49]](#cite_note-67)
* Some listed phones are not known to exist as [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") in any language.

#### Non-pulmonic consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Non-pulmonic consonants")]

Non-pulmonic consonants are sounds whose airflow is not dependent on the lungs. These include [clicks](/wiki/Click_consonant "Click consonant") (found in the [Khoisan languages](/wiki/Khoisan_languages "Khoisan languages") and some neighboring [Bantu languages](/wiki/Bantu_languages "Bantu languages") of Africa), [implosives](/wiki/Implosives "Implosives") (found in languages such as [Sindhi](/wiki/Sindhi_language "Sindhi language"), [Hausa](/wiki/Hausa_language "Hausa language"), [Swahili](/wiki/Swahili_language "Swahili language") and [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language")), and [ejectives](/wiki/Ejectives "Ejectives") (found in many [Amerindian](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_the_Americas "Indigenous languages of the Americas") and [Caucasian languages](/wiki/Caucasian_languages "Caucasian languages")).

**Notes**

* Clicks have traditionally been described as consisting of a forward place of articulation, commonly called the click "type" or historically the "influx", and a rear place of articulation, which when combined with the quality of the click is commonly called the click "accompaniment" or historically the "efflux". The IPA click letters indicate only the click type (forward articulation and release). Therefore, all clicks require two letters for proper notation: ⟨k͡ǀ, ɡ͡ǀ, q͡ǀ⟩, etc., or with the order reversed if both the forward and rear releases are audible. The letter for the rear articulation is frequently omitted, in which case a ⟨k⟩ may usually be assumed. However, some researchers dispute the idea that clicks should be analyzed as doubly articulated, as the traditional transcription implies, and analyze the rear occlusion as solely a part of the airstream mechanism.[[50]](#cite_note-68) In transcriptions of such approaches, the click letter represents both places of articulation, with the different letters representing the different click types, and diacritics are used for the elements of the accompaniment: ⟨ǀ, ǀ̬, ǀ̃⟩, etc.
* Letters for the [voiceless](/wiki/Voiceless "Voiceless") implosives ⟨ƥ, ƭ, ƈ, ƙ, ʠ⟩ are no longer supported by the IPA, though they remain in Unicode. Instead, the IPA typically uses the voiced equivalent with a voiceless diacritic: ⟨ɓ̥, ɗ̥⟩, etc.
* The letter for the [retroflex implosive](/wiki/Retroflex_implosive "Retroflex implosive"), ⟨ᶑ ⟩, is not "explicitly IPA approved",[[51]](#cite_note-69) but the IPA has endorsed the inclusion of ⟨ᶑ ⟩ and voiceless ⟨𝼉⟩ into Unicode.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* The ejective diacritic is placed at the right-hand margin of the consonant, rather than immediately after the letter for the stop: ⟨t͜ʃʼ⟩, ⟨kʷʼ⟩. In imprecise transcription, it often stands in for a superscript glottal stop in [glottalized](/wiki/Glottalized "Glottalized") but pulmonic [sonorants](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant"), such as [mˀ], [lˀ], [wˀ], [aˀ] – also transcribable as creaky [m̰], [l̰], [w̰], [a̰].

#### Affricates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Affricates")]

[Affricates](/wiki/Affricates "Affricates") and [co-articulated](/wiki/Doubly_articulated_consonant "Doubly articulated consonant") stops are represented by two letters in sequence. For clarity, this [digraph](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") may be joined by a tie bar, which may appear either above or below the letters with no difference in meaning.[[note 19]](#cite_note-71) Affricates are optionally represented by [ligatures](/wiki/Typographic_ligature "Typographic ligature") – e.g. ⟨ʧ, ʤ ⟩ – though this is no longer official IPA usage.[[1]](#cite_note-IPA_1999-1) Alternatively, a superscript notation for a consonant release is sometimes used to transcribe affricates, for example ⟨tˢ⟩ for [t͜s], although in precise notation this would indicate a fricative release rather than an affricate. The letters for the palatal plosives ⟨c⟩ and ⟨ɟ⟩ are often used as a convenience for [t͜ʃ] and [d͜ʒ] or similar affricates, even in official IPA publications, so they must be interpreted with care.[[53]](#cite_note-International_Phonetic_Association_1999_133-72)

Because in a true affricate the plosive element and the fricative element are homorganic, and the place of articulation of an affricate is most audible in the fricative element, the letter for the former will not always be precisely transcribed where such precision would be redundant. For example, while the English *ch* sound is [t̠͡ʃ] in close transcription, the diacritic is commonly left off, for [t͡ʃ]. Similarly, [ʈ͡ʂ] and [ɖ͡ʐ] are more commonly written [t͡ʂ] and [d͡ʐ], and in the ligatures there is only a single retroflex hook.

#### Co-articulated consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Co-articulated consonants")]

[Co-articulated consonants](/wiki/Co-articulated_consonant "Co-articulated consonant") are sounds that involve two simultaneous [places of articulation](/wiki/Places_of_articulation "Places of articulation") (are pronounced using two parts of the [vocal tract](/wiki/Vocal_tract "Vocal tract")). In English, the [w] in "went" is a coarticulated consonant, being pronounced by rounding the lips and raising the back of the tongue. Similar sounds are [ʍ] and [ɥ]. In some languages, plosives can be double-articulated, for example in the name of [Laurent Gbagbo](/wiki/Laurent_Gbagbo "Laurent Gbagbo").

**Notes**

* [ɧ], the [Swedish *sj*-sound](/wiki/Sj-sound "Sj-sound"), is described by the IPA as a "simultaneous [ʃ] and [x]", but it is unlikely such a simultaneous fricative actually exists in any language.[[54]](#cite_note-73)
* Multiple tie bars can be used: ⟨a͡b͡c⟩ or ⟨a͜b͜c⟩. For instance, a pre-voiced velar affricate may be transcribed as ⟨g͡k͡x⟩.
* If a diacritic needs to be placed on or under a tie bar, the [combining grapheme joiner](/wiki/Combining_grapheme_joiner "Combining grapheme joiner") (U+034F) needs to be used, as in [b͜͏̰də̀bdʊ̀] 'chewed' ([Margi](/wiki/Margi_language "Margi language")). Font support is spotty, however.

With the implosives, authors may not bother to redundantly mark both letters as implosive, but instead write them as less-cluttered ⟨ɡ͡ɓ⟩ and even ⟨k͜ƥ⟩.

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Vowels")]

Main article: [Vowel](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel")

See also: [Cardinal vowels](/wiki/Cardinal_vowels "Cardinal vowels"), [Vowel diagram](/wiki/Vowel_diagram "Vowel diagram"), and [IPA vowel chart with audio](/wiki/IPA_vowel_chart_with_audio "IPA vowel chart with audio")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Cardinal_vowel_tongue_position-front.svg/250px-Cardinal_vowel_tongue_position-front.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cardinal_vowel_tongue_position-front.svg)

Tongue positions of [cardinal](/wiki/Cardinal_vowel "Cardinal vowel") front vowels, with highest point indicated. The position of the highest point is used to determine vowel height and backness.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Cardinal_vowels-Jones_x-ray.jpg/250px-Cardinal_vowels-Jones_x-ray.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cardinal_vowels-Jones_x-ray.jpg)

[X-ray photos](/wiki/Radiography "Radiography") show the sounds [i, u, a, ɑ].

The IPA defines a vowel as a sound which occurs at a syllable center.[[55]](#cite_note-74) Below is a chart depicting the vowels of the IPA. The IPA maps the vowels according to the position of the tongue.

The vertical axis of the chart is mapped by [vowel height](/wiki/Vowel_height "Vowel height"). Vowels pronounced with the tongue lowered are at the bottom, and vowels pronounced with the tongue raised are at the top. For example, [ɑ] (the first vowel in *father*) is at the bottom because the tongue is lowered in this position. [i] (the vowel in "meet") is at the top because the sound is said with the tongue raised to the roof of the mouth.

In a similar fashion, the horizontal axis of the chart is determined by [vowel backness](/wiki/Vowel_backness "Vowel backness"). Vowels with the tongue moved towards the front of the mouth (such as [ɛ], the vowel in "met") are to the left in the chart, while those in which it is moved to the back (such as [ʌ], the vowel in "but") are placed to the right in the chart.

In places where vowels are paired, the right represents a [rounded vowel](/wiki/Rounded_vowel "Rounded vowel") (in which the lips are rounded) while the left is its unrounded counterpart.

#### Diphthongs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Diphthongs")]

[Diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") may be written as simple sequences of letters, but for clarity they are commonly specified with a non-syllabic diacritic, as in ⟨ui̯⟩ or ⟨u̯i⟩, or with a superscript for the on- or off-glide, as in ⟨uⁱ⟩ or ⟨ᵘi⟩. Sometimes a tie bar is used: ⟨u͜i⟩, especially when it is difficult to tell if the diphthong is characterized by an on-glide or an off-glide, or when it is variable.

Notes

* ⟨a⟩ officially represents a front vowel, but there is little if any distinction between front and central open vowels (see [Vowel § Acoustics](/wiki/Vowel#Acoustics "Vowel")), and ⟨a⟩ is frequently used for an open central vowel.[[31]](#cite_note-thomason-42) If disambiguation is required, the [retraction diacritic](/wiki/Retraction_(phonetics) "Retraction (phonetics)") or the [centralized diacritic](/wiki/Relative_articulation#Centralized_vowels "Relative articulation") may be added to indicate an open central vowel, as in ⟨a̠⟩ or ⟨ä⟩.

## Diacritics and prosodic notation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Diacritics and prosodic notation")]

[Diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") are used for phonetic detail. They are added to IPA letters to indicate a modification or specification of that letter's normal pronunciation.[[56]](#cite_note-IPA15-75)

By being made superscript, any IPA letter may function as a diacritic, conferring elements of its articulation to the base letter.[[57]](#cite_note-non-pulmonic-76) Those superscript letters listed below are specifically provided for by the IPA *Handbook*; other uses can be illustrated with ⟨tˢ⟩ ([t] with fricative release), ⟨ᵗs⟩ ([s] with affricate onset), ⟨ⁿd⟩ (prenasalized [d]), ⟨bʱ⟩ ([b] with breathy voice), ⟨mˀ⟩ (glottalized [m]), ⟨sᶴ⟩ ([s] with a flavor of [ʃ], i.e. a [voiceless alveolar retracted sibilant](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_retracted_sibilant "Voiceless alveolar retracted sibilant")), ⟨oᶷ⟩ ([o] with [diphthongization](/wiki/Diphthongization "Diphthongization")), ⟨ɯᵝ⟩ ([compressed](/wiki/Compressed_vowel "Compressed vowel") [ɯ]). Superscript diacritics placed after a letter are ambiguous between simultaneous modification of the sound and phonetic detail at the end of the sound. For example, labialized ⟨kʷ⟩ may mean either simultaneous [k] and [w] or else [k] with a labialized release. Superscript diacritics placed before a letter, on the other hand, normally indicate a modification of the onset of the sound (⟨mˀ⟩ glottalized [m], ⟨ˀm⟩ [m] with a glottal onset). (See [§ Superscript IPA](#Superscript_IPA).)

Many linguists prefer one of the diacritics dedicated to breathy voice over simple aspiration, such as ⟨b̤⟩. Some linguists restrict that diacritic to [sonorants](/wiki/Sonorant "Sonorant"), such as breathy-voice ⟨m̤⟩, and transcribe voiced-aspirated obstruents as e.g. ⟨bʱ⟩.
2. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-u17_78-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-u17_78-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-u17_78-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-u17_78-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-u17_78-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-u17_78-5) [***g***](#cite_ref-u17_78-6) [***h***](#cite_ref-u17_78-7) [***i***](#cite_ref-u17_78-8) These diacritics-above were added to Unicode in September 2025. It may be some time before there is widespread font support.
3. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-spacingdiacritics_79-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-spacingdiacritics_79-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-spacingdiacritics_79-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-spacingdiacritics_79-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-spacingdiacritics_79-4) [***f***](#cite_ref-spacingdiacritics_79-5) The raising, lowering, advanced and retracted diacritics are sometimes seen with a third placement option, as spacing diacritics ⟨˔⟩, ⟨˕⟩, ⟨˖⟩, ⟨˗⟩ that avoid both descenders and ascenders, as seen here on ⟨ɭ˔⟩. Officially, however, these were retired from the IPA with the 1989 Kiel convention. Spacing ⟨◌꭪⟩, ⟨◌꭫⟩ were used similarly when they meant advanced and retracted, and they are occasionally seen with their modern values.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-retracted_80-0)** Care must be taken that a superscript retraction sign is not mistaken for a macron for mid tone.
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-lessrounded_81-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-lessrounded_81-1) These indicate position on a cline from rounded to spread relative to the cardinal value of the letter. They can thus be applied to unrounded vowels: [ɛ̜] is more spread than cardinal [ɛ], and [ɯ̹] is less spread (more rounded) than cardinal [ɯ].

A diacritic may be moved to avoid conflicts of space. One that is normally placed below a letter may be moved above it to avoid a [descender](/wiki/Descender "Descender") or another diacritic, as with the voiceless ring on ⟨ŋ̊⟩, and vice versa with the breve or the tie bar on ⟨t͜s⟩, though the tie bar is basically in free variation.[[56]](#cite_note-IPA15-75) Exceptions are the tilde, [trema](/wiki/Two_dots_(diacritic) "Two dots (diacritic)") and [caron](/wiki/Caron "Caron")/wedge – and, in extIPA, the bridge – which are defined differently when placed above and below a letter.

A couple additional superscript letters are found for secondary articulation. In the *Handbook*, for example, ⟨ʱ⟩ is used for voiced aspiration. ⟨ᶣ⟩ is commonly seen with languages such as [Twi](/wiki/Twi "Twi") where consonants may be simultaneously palatalized and labialized, while ⟨ˀ⟩ may be used for glottalized sounds without specifying whether they are ejective or have [creaky voice](/wiki/Creaky_voice "Creaky voice"). ExtIPA provides ⟨ʶ⟩ for [uvularization](/wiki/Uvularization "Uvularization"), and the [Voice Quality Symbols](/wiki/Voice_Quality_Symbols "Voice Quality Symbols") provide a couple more. However, only limited set of IPA letters are used in this fashion; for all others, superscripting indicates more ambiguous shading of the sound.

The state of the [glottis](/wiki/Glottis "Glottis") can be finely transcribed with diacritics. A series of alveolar plosives ranging from open-glottis to closed-glottis [phonation](/wiki/Phonation "Phonation") is:

Phonation scale

| Open glottis | [t] | [voiceless](/wiki/Voiceless "Voiceless") |

|  | [d̥] | [slack voice](/wiki/Slack_voice "Slack voice") |
| [Sweet spot](/wiki/Sweet_spot_(phonetics) "Sweet spot (phonetics)") | [d] | [modal voice](/wiki/Modal_voice "Modal voice") |
|  | [d̬] | [stiff voice](/wiki/Stiff_voice "Stiff voice") |
|  | [d̰] | [creaky voice](/wiki/Creaky_voice "Creaky voice") |
| Closed glottis | [ʔ͜t] | glottal closure |

Additional diacritics are provided by the [Extensions to the IPA](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_IPA "Extensions to the IPA") for speech pathology.

### Suprasegmentals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Suprasegmentals")]

These symbols describe the features of a language above the [level](/wiki/Phonological_hierarchy "Phonological hierarchy") of individual consonants and vowels, that is, at the level of syllable, word or [phrase](/wiki/Phrase "Phrase"). These include [prosody](/wiki/Prosody_(linguistics) "Prosody (linguistics)"), pitch, [length](/wiki/Length_(phonetics) "Length (phonetics)"), [stress](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)"), intensity, [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") and gemination of the sounds of a language, as well as the [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm") and [intonation](/wiki/Intonation_(linguistics) "Intonation (linguistics)") of speech.[[59]](#cite_note-Handbook13-83) Various ligatures of pitch/tone letters and diacritics are provided for by the [Kiel Convention](/wiki/Kiel_Convention "Kiel Convention") and used in the IPA *Handbook* despite not being found in the summary of the IPA alphabet found on the one-page chart.

Under [capital letters](#Capital_letters) below we will see how a carrier letter may be used to indicate suprasegmental features such as labialization or nasalization. Some authors omit the carrier letter, for e.g. suffixed [kʰuˣt̪s̟]ʷ or prefixed [ʷkʰuˣt̪s̟],[[note 20]](#cite_note-84) or place a spacing variant of a diacritic such as ⟨˔⟩ or ⟨˜⟩ at the beginning or end of a word to indicate that it applies to the entire word.[[note 21]](#cite_note-85)
