#### Stress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Stress")]

Officially, the [stress marks](/wiki/Stress_(linguistics) "Stress (linguistics)") ⟨ˈ ˌ⟩ appear before the stressed syllable, and thus mark the syllable boundary as well as stress (though the syllable boundary may still be explicitly marked with a period).[[63]](#cite_note-report75-76-95) Occasionally the stress mark is placed immediately before the nucleus of the syllable, after any consonantal onset.[[64]](#cite_note-96) In such transcriptions, the stress mark does not mark a syllable boundary. The primary stress mark may be [doubled](#Comparative_degree) ⟨ˈˈ⟩ for extra stress (such as prosodic stress). The secondary stress mark is sometimes seen doubled ⟨ˌˌ⟩ for extra-weak stress, but this convention has not been adopted by the IPA.[[63]](#cite_note-report75-76-95) Some dictionaries place both stress marks before a syllable, ⟨¦⟩, to indicate that pronunciations with either primary or secondary stress are heard, though this is not IPA usage.[[note 27]](#cite_note-97)

#### Boundary markers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Boundary markers")]

There are three boundary markers: ⟨.⟩ for a syllable break, ⟨|⟩ for a minor prosodic break and ⟨‖⟩ for a major prosodic break. The tags 'minor' and 'major' are intentionally ambiguous. Depending on need, 'minor' may vary from a [foot](/wiki/Foot_(prosody) "Foot (prosody)") break to a break in list-intonation to a continuing–prosodic unit boundary (equivalent to a comma), and while 'major' is often any intonation break, it may be restricted to a final–prosodic unit boundary (equivalent to a period). The 'major' symbol may also be doubled, ⟨‖‖⟩, for a stronger break.[[note 28]](#cite_note-99)

Although not part of the IPA, the following additional boundary markers are often used in conjunction with the IPA: ⟨μ⟩ for a [mora](/wiki/Mora_(linguistics) "Mora (linguistics)") or mora boundary, ⟨σ⟩ for a syllable or syllable boundary, ⟨+⟩ for a morpheme boundary, ⟨#⟩ for a word boundary (may be doubled, ⟨##⟩, for e.g. a breath-group boundary),[[66]](#cite_note-100) ⟨$⟩ for a phrase or intermediate boundary and ⟨%⟩ for a prosodic boundary. For example, C# is a word-final consonant, %V a post-[pausa](/wiki/Pausa "Pausa") vowel, and σC a syllable-initial consonant.

#### Pitch and tone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Pitch and tone")]

See also: [tone letter](/wiki/Tone_letter "Tone letter")

⟨ꜛ ꜜ⟩ are defined in the *Handbook* as "upstep" and "downstep", concepts from tonal languages. However, the upstep symbol can also be used for [pitch reset](/wiki/Pitch_reset "Pitch reset"), and the IPA *Handbook* uses it for prosody in the illustration for Portuguese, a non-tonal language.

Phonetic pitch and phonemic tone may be indicated by either diacritics placed over the nucleus of the syllable – e.g., high-pitch ⟨é⟩ – or by [Chao tone letters](/wiki/Chao_tone_letter "Chao tone letter") placed either before or after the word or syllable. There are three graphic variants of the tone letters: with or without a stave, and facing left or facing right from the stave. The stave was introduced with the 1989 Kiel Convention, as was the option of placing a staved letter after the word or syllable, while retaining the older conventions. There are therefore six ways to transcribe pitch/tone in the IPA: i.e., ⟨é⟩, ⟨˦e⟩, ⟨e˦⟩, ⟨꜓e⟩, ⟨e꜓⟩ and ⟨ˉe⟩ for a high pitch/tone.[[63]](#cite_note-report75-76-95)[[67]](#cite_note-101)[[68]](#cite_note-102) Of the tone letters, only left-facing staved letters and a few representative combinations are shown in the summary on the *Chart*, and in practice it is currently more common for tone letters to occur after the syllable/word than before, as in the Chao tradition. Placement before the word is a carry-over from the pre-Kiel IPA convention, as is still the case for the stress and upstep/downstep marks. The IPA endorses the Chao tradition of using the left-facing tone letters, ⟨˥ ˦ ˧ ˨ ˩⟩, for underlying tone, and the right-facing letters, ⟨꜒ ꜓ ꜔ ꜕ ꜖⟩, for surface tone, as occurs in [tone sandhi](/wiki/Tone_sandhi "Tone sandhi"), and for the intonation of non-tonal languages. In the Portuguese illustration in the 1999 *Handbook*, for example, tone letters are placed before a word or syllable to indicate prosodic pitch (equivalent to [↗︎] global rise and [↘︎] global fall, but allowing more precision), and in the Cantonese illustration they are placed after a word/syllable to indicate lexical tone. Theoretically therefore prosodic pitch and lexical tone could be simultaneously transcribed in a single text, though this is not a formalized distinction.

Rising and falling pitch, as in [contour tones](/wiki/Contour_tone "Contour tone"), are indicated by combining the pitch diacritics and letters in the table, such as grave plus acute for rising [ě] and acute plus grave for falling [ê]. Only the six combinations of two diacritics are listed in the IPA chart and *Handbook*, across just three basic pitch levels (high, mid, low), despite the doubled diacritics supporting five levels of pitch in isolation. The four other explicitly approved rising and falling diacritic combinations are high/mid rising [e᷄], low rising [e᷅], high falling [e᷇], and low/mid falling [e᷆].[[note 29]](#cite_note-103) Combinations with double grave and double acute are occasionally encountered in the literature,[[note 30]](#cite_note-108) but apart from grave–double acute are not supported by Unicode.

The Chao tone letters, on the other hand, may be combined in any pattern, and are therefore used for more complex contours and finer distinctions than the diacritics allow, such as mid-rising [e˨˦], extra-high falling [e˥˦], etc. There are 20 such possibilities. However, in Chao's original proposal, which was adopted by the IPA in 1989, he stipulated that the half-high and half-low letters ⟨˦ ˨⟩ may be combined with each other, but not with the other three tone letters, so as not to create spuriously precise distinctions. With this restriction, there are 8 possibilities.[[73]](#cite_note-Chao-109)

The old staveless tone letters tend to be more restricted than the staved letters, though not as restricted as the diacritics. Technically they support as many distinctions as the staved letters,[[note 31]](#cite_note-110) but in the decades prior to the Kiel Convention only three pitch levels were provided for level tones, and only two for contour tones. Unicode supports default or high-pitch ⟨ˉ ˊ ˋ ˆ ˇ ˜ ˙⟩ and low-pitch ⟨ˍ ˏ ˎ ꞈ ˬ ˷⟩. Only one mid-pitch tone is currently supported, the mid-level grave ⟨˴⟩; the acute equivalent is provisionally assigned in Unicode as of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit),[[74]](#cite_note-111) while the mid-macron was judged too similar to other hyphen-like symbols to receive encoding. The IPA had also used dots for [neutral tones](/wiki/Neutral_tone "Neutral tone"), but the corresponding dotted Chao tone letters were not adopted at the Kiel Convention.

Although tone diacritics and tone letters are presented as equivalent on the chart, "this was done only to simplify the layout of the chart. The two sets of symbols are not comparable in this way."[[75]](#cite_note-112) Using diacritics, a high tone is ⟨é⟩ and a low tone is ⟨è⟩; in tone letters, these are ⟨e˥⟩ and ⟨e˩⟩. One can double the diacritics for extra-high ⟨e̋⟩ and extra-low ⟨ȅ⟩; there is no parallel to this using tone letters. Instead, tone letters have mid-high ⟨e˦⟩ and mid-low ⟨e˨⟩; again, there is no equivalent among the diacritics. Thus in a three-register tone system, ⟨é ē è⟩ are equivalent to ⟨e˥ e˧ e˩⟩, while in a four-register system, ⟨e̋ é è ȅ⟩ may be equivalent to ⟨e˥ e˦ e˨ e˩⟩.[[63]](#cite_note-report75-76-95)

The correspondence breaks down even further once they start combining. For more complex tones, one may combine three or four tone diacritics in any permutation,[[63]](#cite_note-report75-76-95) though in practice only generic peaking (rising-falling) e᷈ and dipping (falling-rising) e᷉ combinations are used. Chao tone letters are required for finer detail (e˧˥˧, e˩˨˩, e˦˩˧, e˨˩˦, etc.). Although only 10 peaking and dipping tones were proposed in Chao's original, limited set of tone letters, phoneticians often make finer distinctions, and indeed an example is found on the IPA Chart.[[note 32]](#cite_note-113) The system allows the transcription of 112 peaking and dipping pitch contours, including tones that are level for part of their length.

Original (restricted) set of Chao tone letters[[note 33]](#cite_note-114)

| Register | Level [[note 34]](#cite_note-115) | Rising | Falling | Peaking | Dipping |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| e˩ | e˩˩ | e˩˧ | e˧˩ | e˩˧˩ | e˧˩˧ |
| e˨ | e˨˨ | e˨˦ | e˦˨ | e˨˦˨ | e˦˨˦ |
| e˧ | e˧˧ | e˧˥ | e˥˧ | e˧˥˧ | e˥˧˥ |
| e˦ | e˦˦ |  |  | e˧˥˩ | e˧˩˥ |
| e˥ | e˥˥ | e˩˥ | e˥˩ | e˩˥˧ | e˥˩˧ |

More complex contours are possible. Chao gave an example of [꜔꜒꜖꜔] (mid-high-low-mid) from English prosody.[[73]](#cite_note-Chao-109)

Chao tone letters generally appear after each syllable, for a language with syllable tone (e.g. ⟨a˧vɔ˥˩⟩) or after the phonological word, for a language with [word tone](/wiki/Word_tone "Word tone") (e.g. ⟨avɔ˧˥˩⟩ for the same change in pitch). The IPA gives the option of placing the tone letters before the word or syllable (⟨˧a˥˩vɔ⟩, ⟨˧˥˩avɔ⟩), and illustrates this for prosody, but it is rare for lexical tone. Reversed tone letters may be used to clarify that they apply to the following rather than to the preceding syllable (⟨꜔a꜒꜖vɔ⟩, ⟨꜔꜒꜖avɔ⟩). The staveless letters are not directly supported by Unicode, but some fonts allow the stave in Chao tone letters to be suppressed.

### Comparative degree

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Comparative degree")]

IPA diacritics may be doubled to indicate an extra degree (greater intensity) of the feature indicated.[[76]](#cite_note-K&L-116) This is a productive process, but apart from extra-high and extra-low tones being marked by doubled high- and low-tone diacritics, ⟨ə̋, ə̏⟩, the major [prosodic break](/wiki/Prosodic_unit "Prosodic unit") ⟨**‖**⟩ being marked as a doubled minor break ⟨**|**⟩, and a couple other instances, such usage is not enumerated by the IPA.

For example, the stress mark may be doubled to indicate an extra degree of stress, such as prosodic stress in English;[[77]](#cite_note-117) the stress mark and prosodic-break bar may even tripled, with ⟨ˈˈˈ⟩ and ⟨**⦀**⟩ for even greater intensity.[[78]](#cite_note-118)[[79]](#cite_note-119) An example in French, with a single stress mark for normal prosodic stress at the end of each [prosodic unit](/wiki/Prosodic_unit "Prosodic unit") (marked as a minor prosodic break), and a double stress mark for contrastive/emphatic stress: [ˈˈɑ̃ːˈtre **|** məˈsjø **‖** ˈˈvwala maˈdam **‖**] **Entrez monsieur, voilà madame*.*[[80]](#cite_note-120) Similarly, a doubled secondary stress mark ⟨ˌˌ⟩ has been used for tertiary (extra-light) stress, though a proposal to officially adopt this was rejected.[[81]](#cite_note-121) In a similar vein, the effectively obsolete staveless tone letters were once doubled for an emphatic rising intonation ⟨˶⟩ and an emphatic falling intonation ⟨˵⟩.[[82]](#cite_note-122)

[Length](/wiki/Length_(phonetics) "Length (phonetics)") is commonly extended by repeating the length marks, e.g. [ĕ e eˑ eː eːˑ eːː] *etc*. Such usage may be phonetic, as in English *shhh!* [ʃːːː], or phonemic, as in the "overlong" segments of [Estonian](/wiki/Estonian_phonology "Estonian phonology"):

* *vere* /vere/ 'blood [gen.sg.]', *veere* /veːre/ 'edge [gen.sg.]', *veere* /veːːre/ 'roll [imp. 2nd sg.]'
* *lina* /linɑ/ 'sheet', *linna* /linːɑ/ 'town [gen. sg.]', *linna* /linːːɑ/ 'town [ill. sg.]'

(Normally additional phonemic degrees of length are handled by the extra-short or half-long diacritic, i.e. ⟨e eˑ eː⟩ or ⟨ĕ e eː⟩, but the first two words in each of the Estonian examples are analyzed as typically short and long, /e eː/ and /n nː/, requiring a different remedy for the additional words.)

[Delimiters](#Delimiters) are similar: double slashes indicate extra phonemic (morpho-phonemic), double square brackets especially precise transcription, and double parentheses especially unintelligible.

Occasionally other diacritics are doubled:

* [Rhoticity](/wiki/R-colored_vowel "R-colored vowel"), as in [Badaga](/wiki/Badaga_language "Badaga language") non-rhotic /be/ "mouth", slightly rhotic /be˞/ "bangle", and strongly rhotic /be˞˞/ "crop".[[83]](#cite_note-123)
* Mild and strong [aspiration](/wiki/Aspiration_(phonetics) "Aspiration (phonetics)"), as in [kʰ] vs [kʰʰ].[[note 35]](#cite_note-124)
* [Nasalization](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel"), as in [Palantla Chinantec](/wiki/Palantla_Chinantec "Palantla Chinantec") lightly nasalized /ẽ/ vs heavily nasalized /ẽ̃/;[[84]](#cite_note-125) in documents from before 2015 some care may be needed to distinguish this from the old [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA") diacritic for [velopharyngeal frication](/wiki/Velopharyngeal_frication "Velopharyngeal frication") in disordered speech, ⟨e͌⟩, which has also been analyzed as extreme nasalization.
* Weak vs strong [ejectives](/wiki/Ejective "Ejective"), as in [kʼ] vs [kˮ].[[85]](#cite_note-126)
* Especially lowered, such as [t̞̞] (or [t˕˕] if space is tight) for /t/ as a weak fricative in some pronunciations of *register*.[[86]](#cite_note-127)
* Especially retracted, as in [ø̠̠] or [s̠̠],[[note 36]](#cite_note-128)[[76]](#cite_note-K&L-116)[[87]](#cite_note-129) though some care might be needed to distinguish this from indications of alveolar or alveolarized articulation in [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA"), e.g. [s͇].
* Especially advanced, as in [u᫊], or raised, as in [a̝̝] (or [a˔˔] if space is tight).[[76]](#cite_note-K&L-116)
* A greater degree of centralization, perhaps implying a distinction between a merely centralized vowel and a true central vowel, e.g. [ʊ̈] vs [ʊ̈̈].
* Especially guttural, as in [ɫ] (velarized l) and [ꬸ] (pharyngealized l).[[88]](#cite_note-130)
* The transcription of [strident](/wiki/Strident_vowel "Strident vowel") and [harsh voice](/wiki/Harsh_voice "Harsh voice") as extra-creaky /a᷽/ may be motivated by the similarities of these phonations.

The [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA") provides combining parentheses for weak intensity, which when combined with a doubled diacritic indicate an intermediate degree. For instance, increasing degrees of nasalization of the vowel [e] might be written ⟨e ẽ᪻ ẽ ẽ̃᪻ ẽ̃⟩.

## Brackets and transcription delimiters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Brackets and transcription delimiters")]

There are two principal types of [brackets](/wiki/Bracket "Bracket") used to set off (delimit) IPA transcriptions:

| Symbol | Use |
| --- | --- |
| [ ... ] | [Square brackets](/wiki/Square_brackets "Square brackets") are used with [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") notation, whether broad or narrow[[89]](#cite_note-IPA175-131) – that is, for actual pronunciation, possibly including details of the pronunciation that may not be used for distinguishing words in the language being transcribed, but which the author nonetheless wishes to document. Such phonetic notation is the primary function of the IPA. |
| / ... / | [Slashes](/wiki/Slash_(punctuation) "Slash (punctuation)") are used for abstract [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") notation,[[89]](#cite_note-IPA175-131) which note only features that are distinctive in the language, without any extraneous detail. This is typical of dictionaries, such as the *[OED](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*. For example, while the 'p' sounds of English *pin* and *spin* are pronounced differently (and this difference would be meaningful in some languages e.g. [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") and [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese")), the difference is not meaningful in English. Thus, *phonemically* the words are usually analyzed as /ˈpɪn/ and /ˈspɪn/, with the same phoneme /p/. To capture the difference between them – the [allophones](/wiki/Allophone "Allophone") of /p/ – they can be transcribed phonetically as [pʰɪn] and [spɪn]. Phonemic notation commonly uses IPA symbols that are rather close to the default pronunciation of a phoneme, but for legibility often uses simple and 'familiar' letters rather than precise notation, for example /r/ and /o/ for the English sounds [ɹʷ] and [əʊ̯], or /c/, /ɟ/ for [t͜ʃ], [d͜ʒ] as mentioned above.[[13]](#cite_note-principles49-familiar-19) |

Less common conventions include:

| Symbol | Use |
| --- | --- |
| { ... } | [Braces](/wiki/Bracket#Curly_brackets "Bracket") ("curly brackets") are used for [prosodic](/wiki/Prosodic "Prosodic") notation.[[90]](#cite_note-IPA176-132) See [Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet") for examples in this system. |
| ( ... ) | [Parentheses](/wiki/Parentheses "Parentheses") are used for indistinguishable[[89]](#cite_note-IPA175-131) or unidentified utterances. They are also seen for silent articulation (mouthing),[[91]](#cite_note-IPA191-133) where the expected phonetic transcription is derived from lip-reading, and with periods to indicate silent pauses, for example (…) or (2 sec). The latter usage is made official in the [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA"), with unidentified segments circled instead.[[92]](#cite_note-134)  In inventory charts, parentheses may indicate that a sound is a marginal phoneme (occurs in only a few morphemes), is only found in loanwords, or is not a phoneme but a notable allophone.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |
| ⸨ ... ⸩ | [Double parentheses](/wiki/Parenthesis "Parenthesis") indicate either a transcription of obscured speech or a description of the obscuring noise. The IPA specifies that they mark the obscured sound,[[90]](#cite_note-IPA176-132) as in ⸨2σ⸩, two audible syllables obscured by another sound. The current extIPA specifications prescribe double parentheses for the extraneous noise, such as ⸨cough⸩ for a cough by another person (not the speaker) or ⸨knock⸩ for a knock on a door, but the IPA *Handbook* identifies IPA and extIPA usage as equivalent.[[93]](#cite_note-135) Early publications of the extIPA explain double parentheses as marking "uncertainty because of noise which obscures the recording", and that within them "may be indicated as much detail as the transcriber can detect."[[94]](#cite_note-136) |

All three of the above are provided by the IPA *Handbook*. The following are not, but may be seen in IPA transcription or in associated material (especially angle brackets):

| Symbol | Field | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ⟦ ... ⟧ | Phonetics | [Double square brackets](/wiki/Square_bracket "Square bracket") are used for especially precise phonetic transcription, often finer than is normally practicable.[[95]](#cite_note-137) This is consistent with the IPA convention of doubling a symbol to indicate greater degree. Double brackets may indicate that a letter has its cardinal IPA value. For example, ⟦a⟧ is an open front vowel, rather than the perhaps slightly different value (such as open central) that "[a]" may be used to transcribe in a particular language. Thus, two vowels transcribed for easy legibility as [e] and [ɛ] may be clarified as actually being ⟦e̝⟧ and ⟦e⟧; [ð] may be more precisely ⟦ð̠̞ˠ⟧.[[96]](#cite_note-138) Double brackets may also be used for a specific token or speaker; for example, the pronunciation of a particular child as opposed to the adult pronunciation that is their target.[[97]](#cite_note-139) |
| * ⫽ ... ⫽ * { ... } * | ... | * ‖ ... ‖ | [Morphophonology](/wiki/Morphophonology "Morphophonology") | [Double slashes](/wiki/Slash_(punctuation) "Slash (punctuation)") are used for [morphophonemic](/wiki/Morphophonemic "Morphophonemic") transcription. This is also consistent with the IPA convention of doubling a symbol to indicate greater degree – in this case, more abstract than phonemic transcription.  Also commonly seen are the braces of [set theory](/wiki/Set_theory "Set theory"), especially when enclosing the set of phonemes that constitute the morphophoneme, e.g. {t d} or {t|d} or {/t/, /d/} for a conflated /t/ and /d/. Braces have a conflicting use to delimit prosodic transcription within the [Voice Quality Symbols](/wiki/Voice_Quality_Symbols "Voice Quality Symbols"), which are an extension of IPA used in extIPA, but are not otherwise used in IPA proper.  Other delimiters sometimes seen are [pipes](/wiki/Vertical_bar "Vertical bar") and double pipes taken from [Americanist phonetic notation](/wiki/Americanist_phonetic_notation "Americanist phonetic notation"). However, these conflict with the pipes used in basic IPA prosodic transcription.[[note 37]](#cite_note-141) |
| * \ ... \ * ⫽ ... ⫽ * | ... | * ! ... ! | [Diaphonology](/wiki/Diaphonology "Diaphonology") | [Backslashes](/wiki/Backslash "Backslash") are used for [diaphonemic](/wiki/Diaphonemic "Diaphonemic") transcription, for example setting off pronunciations in dictionaries that do not target a specific preferred dialect.[[note 38]](#cite_note-MW-142)  However, backslashes indicate reiterated (stuttered) articulation in extIPA.  Other delimiters are double slashes (the same notation as for morphophonology), exclamation marks, and pipes. |
| * ⟨ ... ⟩ * ⟪ ... ⟫ * | ... | | [Graphemics](/wiki/Graphemics "Graphemics") | [Angle brackets](/wiki/Angle_bracket "Angle bracket")[[note 39]](#cite_note-143) are used to mark both original Latin-script orthography and Latin [transliteration](/wiki/Transliteration "Transliteration") (romanization) of another script; they are also used to identify individual [graphemes](/wiki/Grapheme "Grapheme") of any script.[[99]](#cite_note-144)[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHeselwood20138_ff,_29_ff-145) In IPA literature, they are used to indicate the IPA letters themselves rather than the sound values that they carry.  For example, ⟨cot⟩ would be used for the orthography of the English word *cot*, as opposed to its pronunciation /ˈkɒt/. Italics are usual when words are written as themselves (as with *cot* in the previous sentence) rather than to specifically note their orthography. However, italics are sometimes ambiguous, and italic markup is not always accessible to sight-impaired readers who rely on [screen reader](/wiki/Screen_reader "Screen reader") technology.  Double angle brackets may occasionally be useful to distinguish original orthography from transliteration, or the idiosyncratic spelling of a manuscript from the normalized orthography of the language.  Pipes are sometimes used instead of double angle brackets to denote the distinct [allographs](/wiki/Allograph "Allograph") of a grapheme that are known as *[glyphs](/wiki/Glyphs "Glyphs")*. For example, print |g| and script |ɡ| are two glyph variants of the letter ⟨[g](/wiki/G "G")⟩ of Latin script.[[101]](#cite_note-146) |

Some examples of contrasting brackets in the literature:

> In some English accents, the phoneme /l/, which is usually spelled as ⟨l⟩ or ⟨ll⟩, is articulated as two distinct allophones: the clear [l] occurs before vowels and the consonant /j/, whereas the dark [ɫ]/[lˠ] occurs before consonants, except /j/, and at the end of words.[[102]](#cite_note-147)

> the alternations /f/ – /v/ in plural formation in one class of nouns, as in *knife* /naɪf/ – *knives* /naɪvz/, which can be represented morphophonemically as {naɪV} – {naɪV+z}. The morphophoneme {V} stands for the phoneme set {/f/, /v/}.[[103]](#cite_note-148)

> [ˈf\faɪnəlz ˈhɛld ɪn (.) ⸨knock on door⸩ bɑɹsə{𝑝ˈloʊnə and ˈmədɹɪd 𝑝}] — *f-finals held in Barcelona and Madrid.*[[104]](#cite_note-149)

## Ambiguous letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Ambiguous letters")]

As noted [above](#Description), IPA letters are often used quite loosely in broad transcription if no ambiguity would arise in a particular language. Because of that, IPA letters have not generally been created for sounds that are not distinguished in individual languages. A distinction between voiced fricatives and approximants is only partially implemented by the IPA, for example. Even with the relatively recent addition of the palatal fricative ⟨ʝ⟩ and the velar approximant ⟨ɰ⟩ to the alphabet, other letters, though defined as fricatives, are often ambiguous between fricative and approximant. For forward places, ⟨β⟩ and ⟨ð⟩ can generally be assumed to be fricatives unless they carry a lowering diacritic. Rearward, however, ⟨ʁ⟩ and ⟨ʕ⟩ are perhaps more commonly intended to be approximants even without a lowering diacritic. ⟨h⟩ and ⟨ɦ⟩ are similarly either fricatives or approximants, depending on the language, or even glottal "transitions", without that often being specified in the transcription.

Another common ambiguity is among the letters for palatal consonants. ⟨c⟩ and ⟨ɟ⟩ are not uncommonly used as a typographic convenience for affricates, typically [t͜ʃ] and [d͜ʒ],[[53]](#cite_note-International_Phonetic_Association_1999_133-72) while ⟨ɲ⟩ and ⟨ʎ⟩ are commonly used for palatalized alveolar [n̠ʲ] and [l̠ʲ]. To some extent this may be an effect of analysis, but it is common to match up single IPA letters to the phonemes of a language, without overly worrying about phonetic precision.

It has been argued that the lower-pharyngeal (epiglottal) fricatives ⟨ʜ⟩ and ⟨ʢ⟩ are better characterized as trills, rather than as fricatives that have incidental trilling.[[105]](#cite_note-150) This has the advantage of merging the upper-pharyngeal fricatives [ħ, ʕ] together with the epiglottal plosive [ʡ] and trills [ʜ ʢ] into a single pharyngeal column in the consonant chart. However, in [Shilha Berber](/wiki/Shilha_language "Shilha language") the epiglottal fricatives are not trilled.[[106]](#cite_note-151)[[107]](#cite_note-152) Although they might be transcribed ⟨ħ̠ ʕ̠⟩ to indicate this, the far more common transcription is ⟨ʜ ʢ⟩, which is therefore ambiguous between languages.

Among vowels, ⟨a⟩ is officially a front vowel, but is more commonly treated as a central vowel. The difference, to the extent it is even possible, is not phonemic in any language.

For all phonetic notation, it is good practice for an author to specify exactly what they mean by the symbols that they use.

## Superscript letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Superscript letters")]

Main article: [Unicode subscripts and superscripts § Superscript IPA](/wiki/Unicode_subscripts_and_superscripts#Superscript_IPA "Unicode subscripts and superscripts")

See also: [Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet § Superscript variants](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet#Superscript_variants "Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet")

Superscript IPA letters are used to indicate secondary aspects of articulation. These may be aspects of simultaneous articulation that are considered to be in some sense less dominant than the basic sound, or may be transitional articulations that are interpreted as secondary elements.[[108]](#cite_note-153) Examples include [secondary articulation](/wiki/Secondary_articulation "Secondary articulation"); onsets, releases, aspiration and other transitions; shades of sound; light epenthetic sounds and incompletely articulated sounds. Morphophonemically, superscripts may be used for assimilation, e.g. ⟨aʷ⟩ for the effect of labialization on a vowel /a/, which may be realized as phonemic /o/.[[109]](#cite_note-Wolff-154) The IPA and [ICPLA](/wiki/ICPLA "ICPLA") endorse Unicode encoding of superscript variants of all contemporary segmental letters in the IPA proper and of all additional fricatives in extIPA, including the "implicit" IPA retroflex letters ⟨ꞎ 𝼅 𝼈 ᶑ 𝼊 ⟩.[[29]](#cite_note-pulmonic-40)[[57]](#cite_note-non-pulmonic-76)[[110]](#cite_note-155)

Superscripts are often used as a substitute for the tie bar, for example ⟨tᶴ⟩ for [t͜ʃ] and ⟨kᵖ⟩ or ⟨ᵏp⟩ for [k͜p]. However, in precise notation there is a difference between a fricative release in [tᶴ] and the affricate [t͜ʃ], between a velar onset in [ᵏp] and doubly articulated [k͜p].[[111]](#cite_note-156)

Superscript letters can be meaningfully modified by [combining diacritics](/wiki/Combining_diacritics "Combining diacritics"), just as baseline letters can. For example, a superscript dental nasal in ⟨ⁿ̪d̪⟩, a superscript voiceless velar nasal in ⟨ᵑ̊ǂ⟩, and labial-velar prenasalization in ⟨ᵑ͡ᵐɡ͡b⟩. Although the diacritic may seem a bit oversized compared to the superscript letter it modifies, e.g. ⟨ᵓ̃⟩, this can be an aid to legibility, just as it is with the composite superscript c-cedilla ⟨ᶜ̧⟩ and rhotic vowels ⟨ᵊ˞ ᶟ˞⟩. Superscript length marks can be used to indicate the length of aspiration of a consonant, e.g. [pʰ tʰ𐞂 kʰ𐞁]. Another option is to use extIPA parentheses and a doubled diacritic: ⟨p⁽ʰ⁾ tʰ kʰʰ⟩.[[29]](#cite_note-pulmonic-40)

## Obsolete and nonstandard symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Obsolete and nonstandard symbols")]

Main articles: [Obsolete and nonstandard symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Obsolete_and_nonstandard_symbols_in_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Obsolete and nonstandard symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet"), [Click letter](/wiki/Click_letter "Click letter"), and [Sinological extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Sinological_extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Sinological extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet")

A number of IPA letters and diacritics have been retired or replaced over the years. This number includes duplicate symbols, symbols that were replaced due to user preference, and unitary symbols that were rendered with diacritics or digraphs to reduce the inventory of the IPA. The rejected symbols are now considered obsolete, though some are still seen in the literature.

The IPA once had several pairs of duplicate symbols from alternative proposals, but eventually settled on one or the other. An example is the vowel letter ⟨ɷ⟩, rejected in favor of ⟨ʊ⟩. Affricates were once transcribed with ligatures, such as ⟨ ʦ ʣ ʧ ʤ ⟩ (and others, some of which are not found in Unicode). These have been officially retired but are still used. Letters for specific combinations of primary and secondary articulation have also been mostly retired, with the idea that such features should be indicated with tie bars or diacritics: ⟨ƍ⟩ for [zʷ] is one. In addition, the rare voiceless implosives, ⟨ƥ ƭ ƈ ƙ ʠ ⟩, were dropped soon after their introduction and are now usually written ⟨ɓ̥ ɗ̥ ʄ̊ ɠ̊ ʛ̥ ⟩. The original set of click letters, ⟨ʇ, ʗ, ʖ, ʞ⟩, was retired but is still sometimes seen, as the current pipe letters ⟨ǀ, ǃ, ǁ, ǂ⟩ can cause problems with legibility, especially when used with brackets ([ ] or / /), the letter ⟨l⟩ (small L), or the [prosodic](/wiki/Prosodic "Prosodic") marks ⟨|, ‖⟩. (For this reason, some publications which use the current IPA pipe letters disallow IPA brackets.)[[112]](#cite_note-157)

Individual non-IPA letters may find their way into publications that otherwise use the standard IPA. This is especially common with:

* Affricates, such as the Americanist [barred lambda](/wiki/Barred_lambda "Barred lambda") ⟨ƛ⟩ for [t͜ɬ] or ⟨č⟩ for [t͜ʃ ].[[note 40]](#cite_note-158)
* The [Karlgren](/wiki/Karlgren "Karlgren") letters for Chinese vowels, ⟨ɿ, ʅ , ʮ, ʯ ⟩.
* Digits or combinations of digits and letters for tonal phonemes that have [conventional numbers](/wiki/Tone_number "Tone number") in a local tradition, such as the [four tones of Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese_phonology#Tones "Standard Chinese phonology"). This may be more convenient for comparison between related languages and dialects than a phonetic transcription would be, because tones vary more unpredictably than segmental phonemes do.
* Digits for tone levels, which are simpler to typeset, though the lack of standardization can cause confusion – e.g. ⟨1⟩ is high tone in some languages, but low tone in others; and ⟨3⟩ may be high, medium, or low tone, depending on the local convention.
* Iconic extensions of standard IPA letters that are implicit in the alphabet, such as retroflex consonants ⟨[ᶑ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_implosive "Voiced retroflex implosive")⟩, ⟨[ꞎ](/wiki/Voiceless_retroflex_lateral_fricative "Voiceless retroflex lateral fricative")⟩, ⟨[𝼈](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_lateral_flap "Voiced retroflex lateral flap")⟩, ⟨[𝼊](/wiki/Tenuis_retroflex_click "Tenuis retroflex click")⟩ and central (semi)vowels ⟨ɉ⟩, ⟨𝼾⟩, ⟨𝼿⟩, ⟨ɏ⟩, ⟨ᵻ⟩, ⟨ʏ̵⟩, ⟨ᵿ⟩. The retroflex letters are referred to in the *Handbook* and have been included in Unicode at IPA request.
* Even presidents of the IPA have used para-IPA notation, such as resurrecting the old diacritic ⟨◌̫⟩ for purely labialized sounds (not simultaneously velarized), the lateral fricative letter ⟨ꞎ ⟩, and either the old dot diacritic ⟨ṣ ẓ⟩ or the novel letters ⟨ ᶘ ᶚ⟩ for the not-quite-retroflex fricatives of Polish *sz, ż* and of Russian *ш, ж*.

In addition, it is common to see *ad hoc* typewriter substitutions, generally capital letters, for when IPA support is not available, e.g. S for ⟨ ʃ ⟩. (See also [SAMPA](/wiki/SAMPA "SAMPA") and [X-SAMPA](/wiki/X-SAMPA "X-SAMPA") substitute notation.)

## Extensions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Extensions")]

Main article: [Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/ExtIPA_chart_%282025%29.pdf/page1-500px-ExtIPA_chart_%282025%29.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:ExtIPA_chart_(2025).pdf)

Chart of the Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet (extIPA), as of 2025

The [Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet for Disordered Speech](/wiki/Extensions_to_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet_for_Disordered_Speech "Extensions to the International Phonetic Alphabet for Disordered Speech"), commonly abbreviated "extIPA" and sometimes called "Extended IPA", are symbols whose original purpose was to accurately transcribe [disordered speech](/wiki/Disordered_speech "Disordered speech"). At the [Kiel Convention](/wiki/Kiel_Convention "Kiel Convention") in 1989, a group of linguists drew up the initial extensions,[[note 41]](#cite_note-160) which were based on the previous work of the PRDS (Phonetic Representation of Disordered Speech) Group in the early 1980s.[[114]](#cite_note-161) The extensions were first published in 1990, then modified, and published again in 1994 in the *Journal of the International Phonetic Association*, when they were officially adopted by the [ICPLA](/wiki/ICPLA "ICPLA").[[115]](#cite_note-162) While the original purpose was to transcribe disordered speech, linguists have used the extensions to designate a number of sounds within standard communication, such as hushing, gnashing teeth, and smacking lips,[[2]](#cite_note-world-2) as well as regular lexical sounds such as [lateral fricatives](/wiki/Lateral_fricative "Lateral fricative") that do not have standard IPA symbols.

In addition to the Extensions to the IPA for disordered speech, there are the conventions of the [Voice Quality Symbols](/wiki/Voice_Quality_Symbols "Voice Quality Symbols"), which include a number of symbols for additional airstream mechanisms and secondary articulations in what they call "voice quality".

## Associated notation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Associated notation")]

Capital letters and various characters on the number row of the keyboard are commonly used to extend the alphabet in various ways.

### Associated symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Associated symbols")]

There are various punctuation-like conventions for linguistic transcription that are commonly used together with IPA. Some of the more common are:

⟨\*⟩
:   (a) A [reconstructed form](/wiki/Comparative_linguistics "Comparative linguistics").
:   (b) An [ungrammatical form](/wiki/Grammaticality "Grammaticality") (including an unphonemic form).

⟨\*\*⟩
:   (a) A reconstructed form, deeper (more ancient) than a single ⟨\*⟩, used when reconstructing even further back from already-starred forms.
:   (b) An ungrammatical form. A less common convention than ⟨\*⟩ (b), this is sometimes used when reconstructed and ungrammatical forms occur in the same text.[[116]](#cite_note-163)

⟨×⟩, ⟨✗⟩
:   An ungrammatical form. A less common convention than ⟨\*⟩ (b), this is sometimes used when reconstructed and ungrammatical forms occur in the same text.[[117]](#cite_note-164)

⟨?⟩
:   A doubtfully grammatical form.

⟨%⟩
:   A generalized form, such as a typical shape of a [wanderwort](/wiki/Wanderwort "Wanderwort") that has not actually been reconstructed.[[118]](#cite_note-165)

⟨#⟩
:   A word boundary – e.g. ⟨#V⟩ for a word-initial vowel.

⟨$⟩
:   A [phonological word](/wiki/Phonological_word "Phonological word") boundary; e.g. ⟨H$⟩ for a high tone that occurs in such a position.

⟨+⟩
:   A [morpheme](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme") boundary; e.g. ⫽ˈnɛl+t⫽ for English *knelt*.

⟨\_⟩
:   The location of a segment – e.g. ⟨V\_V⟩ for an intervocalic position, or ⟨\_#⟩ for word-final position.

⟨~⟩
:   Alternation or contrast[[119]](#cite_note-166) – e.g. [f] ~ [v] or [f ~ v] for variation between [f] and [v], noting that a /uː/ ~ /ʊ/ contrast is maintained or lost, or indicating the change of a root in e.g. ⫽ˈniːl ~ ˈnɛl+t⫽ for English *kneel* ~ *knelt*.

⟨∅⟩
:   A null segment or morpheme. This may indicate the absence of an affix, e.g. ⟨kæt-∅⟩ for where an affix might appear but does not (*cat* instead of *cats*), or a deleted segment that leaves a feature behind, such as ⟨∅ʷ⟩ for an theoretical labialized segment that is only realized as labialization on adjacent segments.[[109]](#cite_note-Wolff-154)

### Capital letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Capital letters")]

Full capital letters are not used as IPA symbols, except as typewriter substitutes (e.g. N for ⟨ŋ⟩, S for ⟨ ʃ ⟩, O for ⟨ɔ⟩ – see [SAMPA](/wiki/SAMPA "SAMPA")). They are, however, often used in conjunction with the IPA in two cases:

1. for [(archi)phonemes](/wiki/Archiphoneme "Archiphoneme") and for [natural classes](/wiki/Natural_class "Natural class") of sounds (that is, as wildcards). The [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA") chart, for example, uses capital letters as wildcards in its illustrations.
2. as carrying letters for the [Voice Quality Symbols](/wiki/Voice_Quality_Symbols "Voice Quality Symbols").

Wildcards are commonly used in phonology to summarize syllable or word shapes, or to show the evolution of classes of sounds. For example, the possible syllable shapes of Mandarin can be abstracted as ranging from /V/ (an atonic vowel) to /CGVNᵀ/ (a consonant-glide-vowel-nasal syllable with tone), and [word-final devoicing](/wiki/Word-final_devoicing "Word-final devoicing") may be schematized as C → C̥/\_#. They are also used in [historical linguistics](/wiki/Historical_linguistics "Historical linguistics") for a sound that is posited but whose nature has not been determined beyond some generic category such as {nasal} or {uvular}. In speech pathology, capital letters represent indeterminate sounds, and may be superscripted to indicate they are weakly articulated: e.g. [ᴰ] is a weak indeterminate alveolar, [ᴷ] a weak indeterminate velar.[[120]](#cite_note-167)

There is a degree of variation between authors as to the capital letters used, but these are ubiquitous in English-language material:[[121]](#cite_note-168)

* ⟨C⟩ for {consonant}
* ⟨V⟩ for {vowel}
* ⟨N⟩ for {nasal}

Other common conventions are:[[122]](#cite_note-extipachart-169)

* ⟨T⟩ for {tone/accent} (tonicity)
* ⟨P⟩ for {plosive}
* ⟨F⟩ for {fricative}
* ⟨S⟩ for {sibilant}[[note 42]](#cite_note-170)
* ⟨G⟩ for {glide/semivowel}
* ⟨L⟩ for {lateral} or {liquid}
* ⟨R⟩ for {rhotic} or {resonant/sonorant}[[note 43]](#cite_note-171)
* ⟨Ȼ⟩ for {obstruent}
* ⟨Ʞ⟩ for {click}
* ⟨A, E, Ɨ, O, U⟩ for {open, front, close, back, rounded vowel}[[note 44]](#cite_note-172) and ⟨B, D, Ɉ, C̢, K, Q, Φ, H⟩ for {labial, alveolar, post-alveolar/palatal, retroflex, velar, uvular, pharyngeal, glottal[[note 45]](#cite_note-173) consonant}, respectively
* ⟨X⟩ for {any sound}, as in ⟨CVX⟩ for a heavy syllable {CVC, CVV̯, CVː}

The letters can be modified with IPA diacritics, for example:[[122]](#cite_note-extipachart-169)

* ⟨Cʼ⟩ for {ejective}
* ⟨Ƈ ⟩ for {implosive}
* ⟨N͡C⟩ or ⟨ᴺC⟩ for {prenasalized consonant}
* ⟨Ṽ⟩ for {[nasal vowel](/wiki/Nasal_vowel "Nasal vowel")}
* ⟨CʰV́⟩ for {aspirated CV syllable with high tone}
* ⟨S̬⟩ for {voiced sibilant}
* ⟨N̥⟩ for {voiceless nasal}
* ⟨P͡F⟩ or ⟨Pꟳ⟩ for {affricate}
* ⟨Cᴳ⟩ for a consonant with a glide as secondary articulation (e.g. ⟨Cʲ⟩ for {palatalized consonant} or ⟨Cʷ⟩ for {labialized consonant})
* ⟨D̪⟩ for {dental consonant}

⟨H⟩, ⟨M⟩, ⟨L⟩ are also commonly used for high, mid and low tone, with ⟨LH⟩ for rising tone and ⟨HL⟩ for falling tone, rather than transcribing them overly precisely with IPA tone letters or with ambiguous digits.[[note 46]](#cite_note-174) When distinguishing five levels of pitch, ⟨xH⟩ and ⟨xL⟩ may be used for 'extra high' and 'extra low'. Arbitrary sequences of letters such as ⟨A B C D⟩ may be used for tone phonemes, especially when comparing across related languages where the pitch values of the tones have diverged; [tone numbers](/wiki/Tone_number "Tone number") are also seen in such contexts.

Typical examples of archiphonemic use of capital letters are:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* ⟨I⟩ for the Turkish harmonic vowel set {i y ɯ u}[[note 47]](#cite_note-175)
* ⟨D⟩ for the conflated flapped middle consonant of American English *writer* and *rider*
* ⟨N⟩ for the [homorganic](/wiki/Homorganic "Homorganic") syllable-coda nasal of languages such as Spanish and Japanese (essentially equivalent to the wild-card usage of the letter)
* ⟨R⟩ in cases where a phonemic distinction between trill /r/ and flap /ɾ/ is conflated, as in Spanish *enrejar* /eNreˈxaR/ (the *n* is homorganic and the first *r* is a trill, but the second *r* is variable).[[123]](#cite_note-176)

Similar usage is found for *phonemic* analysis, where a language does not distinguish sounds that have separate letters in the IPA. For instance, [Castillian Spanish](/wiki/Castillian_Spanish "Castillian Spanish") has been analyzed as having phonemes /Θ/ and /S/, which surface as [θ] and [s] in voiceless environments and as [ð] and [z] in voiced environments (e.g. *hazte* /ˈaΘte/ → [ˈaθte], vs *hazme* /ˈaΘme/ → [ˈaðme], or *las manos* /laS ˈmanoS/ → [lazˈmanos]).[[124]](#cite_note-177)

⟨V⟩, ⟨F⟩ and ⟨C⟩ have completely different meanings as [Voice Quality Symbols](/wiki/Voice_Quality_Symbols "Voice Quality Symbols"), where they stand for "voice" (VoQS jargon for [secondary articulation](/wiki/Secondary_articulation "Secondary articulation")),[[note 48]](#cite_note-178) "falsetto" and "creak". These three letters may take diacritics to indicate what kind of voice quality an utterance has, and may be used as carrier letters to extract a suprasegmental feature that occurs on all susceptible segments in a stretch of IPA. For instance, the transcription of [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") [kʷʰuˣʷt̪ʷs̟ʷ] 'cat' and [kʷʰʉˣʷt͜ʃʷ] 'cats' ([Islay](/wiki/Islay "Islay") dialect) can be made more economical by extracting the suprasegmental labialization of the words: Vʷ[kʰuˣt̪s̟] and Vʷ[kʰʉˣt͜ʃ].[[125]](#cite_note-179) The conventional wildcards ⟨X⟩ or ⟨C⟩ might be used instead of VoQS ⟨V⟩ so that the reader does not misinterpret ⟨Vʷ⟩ as meaning that only vowels are labialized (i.e. Xʷ[kʰuˣt̪s̟] for all segments labialized, Cʷ[kʰuˣt̪s̟] for all consonants labialized), or the carrier letter may be omitted altogether (e.g. ʷ[kʰuˣt̪s̟], [ʷkʰuˣt̪s̟] or [kʰuˣt̪s̟]ʷ). (See [§ Suprasegmentals](#Suprasegmentals) for other transcription conventions.)

This summary is to some extent valid internationally, but linguistic material written in other languages may have different associations with capital letters used as wildcards. For example, in German ⟨K⟩ and ⟨V⟩ are used for *Konsonant* 'consonant' and *Vokal* 'vowel'; in Russian, ⟨С⟩ and ⟨Г⟩ are used for согласный (*soglasnyj*, 'consonant') and гласный (*glasnyj*, 'vowel'). In French, tone may be transcribed with ⟨H⟩ and ⟨B⟩ for *haut* 'high' and *bas* 'low';[[126]](#cite_note-180) Russian appears to be the opposite, with ⟨В⟩ for высокий (*vysokij*, 'high') and ⟨Н⟩ for низкий (*nizkij*, 'low').

## Segments without letters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Segments without letters")]

The blank cells on the summary IPA chart can be filled without much difficulty if the need arises.

The missing retroflex letters, namely ⟨ᶑ ꞎ 𝼅 𝼈 𝼊 ⟩, are "implicit" in the alphabet, and the IPA supported their adoption into Unicode.[[29]](#cite_note-pulmonic-40) Attested in the literature are the [retroflex implosive](/wiki/Retroflex_implosive "Retroflex implosive") ⟨ᶑ ⟩, the [voiceless retroflex lateral fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_retroflex_lateral_fricative "Voiceless retroflex lateral fricative") ⟨ꞎ ⟩, the [retroflex lateral flap](/wiki/Retroflex_lateral_flap "Retroflex lateral flap") ⟨𝼈 ⟩ and the [retroflex click](/wiki/Retroflex_click "Retroflex click") ⟨𝼊 ⟩; the first is also mentioned in the IPA *Handbook*, and the lateral fricatives are provided for by the [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA").

The epiglottal trill is arguably covered by the generally trilled epiglottal "fricatives" ⟨ʜ ʢ⟩. Ad hoc letters for near-close central vowels, ⟨ᵻ ᵿ⟩, are used in some descriptions of English, though those are specifically [reduced vowels](/wiki/Reduced_vowels "Reduced vowels") – forming a set with the IPA reduced vowels ⟨ə ɐ⟩ – and the simple points in vowel space are easily transcribed with diacritics: ⟨ɪ̈ ʊ̈⟩ or ⟨ɨ̞ ʉ̞⟩. Diacritics are able to fill in most of the remainder of the charts.[[note 49]](#cite_note-181) If a sound cannot be transcribed, an asterisk ⟨\*⟩ may be used, either as a letter or as a diacritic (as in ⟨k\*⟩ sometimes seen for the [Korean](/wiki/Korean_phonology "Korean phonology") "fortis" velar).

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Consonants")]

Representations of consonant sounds outside of the core set are created by adding diacritics to letters with similar sound values. The Spanish bilabial and dental approximants are commonly written as lowered fricatives, [β̞] and [ð̞] respectively.[[note 50]](#cite_note-182) Similarly, voiced lateral fricatives can be written as raised lateral approximants, [ɭ˔ ʎ̝ ʟ̝], though the extIPA also provides ⟨𝼅⟩ for the first of these. A few languages such as [Banda](/wiki/Banda_languages "Banda languages") have a bilabial flap as the preferred allophone of what is elsewhere a labiodental flap. It has been suggested that this be written with the labiodental flap letter and the advanced diacritic, [ⱱ̟].[[127]](#cite_note-183)
Similarly, the labiodental plosives are now universally [p̪ b̪] (bilabial plosives with the dentalization diacritic) rather than the *ad hoc* letters ⟨ȹ ȸ⟩ once found in Bantuist literature. Other taps can be written as extra-short plosives or laterals, e.g. [ɢ̆ ʟ̆], though in some cases the diacritic would need to be written below the letter. A [retroflex trill](/wiki/Retroflex_trill "Retroflex trill") can be written as a retracted [r̠], just as non-subapical retroflex fricatives and uvular laterals [ʟ̠ q𝼄̠ʼ] sometimes are.

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Vowels")]

The vowels are similarly manageable by using diacritics for raising, lowering, fronting, backing, centering, and mid-centering.[[note 51]](#cite_note-185) For example, the unrounded equivalent of [ʊ] can be transcribed as mid-centered [ɯ̽], and the rounded equivalent of [æ] as raised [ɶ̝] or lowered [œ̞] (though for those who conceive of vowel space as a triangle, simple [ɶ] already is the rounded equivalent of [æ]). True mid vowels are lowered [e̞ ø̞ ɘ̞ ɵ̞ ɤ̞ o̞] or raised [ɛ̝ œ̝ ɜ̝ ɞ̝ ʌ̝ ɔ̝], while centralized [ɪ̈ ʊ̈] and [ä] (or, less commonly, [ɑ̈]) may be used for near-close and open central vowels, respectively, if that would be unambiguous.

The only known vowels that cannot be represented in this scheme are vowels with unexpected [roundedness](/wiki/Roundedness "Roundedness"). For unambiguous transcription, such sounds would require dedicated diacritics. Possibilities include ⟨ʏʷ⟩ or ⟨ɪʷ⟩ for protrusion and ⟨uᵝ⟩ (or VoQS ⟨ɯᶹ⟩) for compression. However, these transcriptions suggest that the sounds are diphthongs, and so while they may be clear for a language like Swedish where they are diphthongs, they may be misleading for languages such as Japanese where they are monophthongs.
The [extIPA](/wiki/ExtIPA "ExtIPA") 'spread' diacritic ⟨◌͍⟩ is sometimes seen for compressed ⟨u͍⟩, ⟨o͍⟩, ⟨ɔ͍⟩, ⟨ɒ͍⟩, though again the intended meaning would need to be explained or they would be interpreted as being spread the way that cardinal ⟦i⟧ is. For protrusion (*w*-like labialization without velarization), Ladefoged & Maddieson use the old IPA omega diacritic for labialization, ⟨◌̫⟩, for protruded ⟨y᫇⟩, ⟨ʏ̫⟩, ⟨ø̫⟩, ⟨œ̫⟩. Its inverse, a turned omega diacritic ⟨◌᫦⟩, was adopted into Unicode in 2025 and is under consideration to mark compression in extIPA.[[129]](#cite_note-186) This is an adaptation of an old IPA convention of rounding an unrounded vowel letter like *i* with a subscript omega and unrounding a rounded letter like *u* with a subscript turned omega.[[130]](#cite_note-187)
Kelly & Local use a similar combining *w* diacritic ⟨◌ᪿ⟩ for protrusion (e.g. ⟨yᷱ øᪿ⟩) and a combining *ʍ* diacritic ⟨◌ᫀ⟩ for compression (e.g. ⟨uᫀ oᫀ⟩).[[131]](#cite_note-188) Because their published transcriptions are manuscript rather than typescript, these are effectively the same symbols as the old IPA diacritics, which indeed are historically cursive *w* and *ʍ*. However, a more angular ⟨◌ᫀ⟩ in typescript might misleadingly suggest that the vowel is protruded and voiceless (like [ʍ]) rather than compressed and voiced.

## Symbol names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Symbol names")]

Main article: [Naming conventions of the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Naming_conventions_of_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Naming conventions of the International Phonetic Alphabet")

In both print and speech, an IPA symbol is often distinguished from the sound it transcribes because IPA letters very often do not have their cardinal IPA values in practice. This is commonly the case in phonemic and broad phonetic transcription, making articulatory descriptions of IPA letters, such as "mid front rounded vowel" or "voiced velar stop", inappropriate as names for those letters. While the *Handbook of the International Phonetic Association* states that no official names exist for its symbols, it admits the presence of one or two common names for each.[[132]](#cite_note-189) The symbols also have [nonce names](/wiki/Nonce_word "Nonce word") in the [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") standard. In many cases, the names in Unicode and the IPA *Handbook* differ. For example, the *Handbook* calls ⟨ɛ⟩ "epsilon", while Unicode calls it "small letter open e".

The traditional names of the Latin and Greek letters are usually used for unmodified letters.[[note 52]](#cite_note-191) Letters which are not directly derived from these alphabets, such as ⟨ʕ⟩, may have a variety of names, sometimes based on the appearance of the symbol or on the sound that it represents. In Unicode, some of the letters of Greek origin have Latin forms for use in IPA; the others use the characters from the Greek block.

For diacritics, there are two methods of naming. For traditional diacritics, the IPA notes the name in a well known language; for example, ⟨é⟩ is "e-[acute](/wiki/Acute_accent "Acute accent")", based on the name of the diacritic in English and French. Non-traditional diacritics are often named after objects they resemble, so ⟨d̪⟩ is called "d-bridge".

[Geoffrey Pullum](/wiki/Geoffrey_Pullum "Geoffrey Pullum") and [William Ladusaw](/w/index.php?title=William_Ladusaw&action=edit&redlink=1 "William Ladusaw (page does not exist)")[[wd](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q127608481#sitelinks-wikipedia "d:Special:EntityPage/Q127608481")] list a variety of names in use for both current and retired IPA symbols in their *[Phonetic Symbol Guide](/wiki/Phonetic_Symbol_Guide "Phonetic Symbol Guide")*. Many of them found their way into Unicode.[[9]](#cite_note-Pullum-11)

## Computer support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Computer support")]

### Unicode

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Unicode")]

Main article: [Phonetic symbols in Unicode § IPA](/wiki/Phonetic_symbols_in_Unicode#IPA "Phonetic symbols in Unicode")

[Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") supports nearly all of the IPA. Apart from basic Latin and Greek and general punctuation, the primary blocks are [IPA Extensions](/wiki/IPA_Extensions "IPA Extensions"), [Spacing Modifier Letters](/wiki/Spacing_Modifier_Letters "Spacing Modifier Letters") and [Combining Diacritical Marks](/wiki/Combining_Diacritical_Marks "Combining Diacritical Marks"), with lesser support from [Phonetic Extensions](/wiki/Phonetic_Extensions "Phonetic Extensions"), [Phonetic Extensions Supplement](/wiki/Phonetic_Extensions_Supplement "Phonetic Extensions Supplement"), [Combining Diacritical Marks Supplement](/wiki/Combining_Diacritical_Marks_Supplement "Combining Diacritical Marks Supplement"), and scattered characters elsewhere. The [extended IPA](/wiki/Extended_IPA "Extended IPA") is supported primarily by those blocks and [Latin Extended-G](/wiki/Latin_Extended-G "Latin Extended-G").

### IPA numbers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: IPA numbers")]

Main article: [IPA number](/wiki/IPA_number "IPA number")

After the [Kiel Convention](/wiki/Kiel_Convention "Kiel Convention") in 1989, most IPA symbols were assigned an identifying number to prevent confusion between similar characters during the printing of manuscripts. The codes were never much used and have been superseded by Unicode.

### Typefaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Typefaces")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/IPA_font_sample_%28SIL%2C_Brill%2C_Noto%2C_DejaVu%2C_TNR%29.svg/250px-IPA_font_sample_%28SIL%2C_Brill%2C_Noto%2C_DejaVu%2C_TNR%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:IPA_font_sample_(SIL,_Brill,_Noto,_DejaVu,_TNR).svg)

The sequence ⟨˨˦˧꜒꜔꜓k͜𝼄a͎̽᷅ꟸ⟩ in the fonts Gentium, Andika, Brill, Noto Serif, DejaVu Sans and Times New Roman. Asterisks are characters not supported by that font. All but DejaVu are able to stack diacritics; in Noto, the arrow diacritic does not align well. In Noto, DejaVu and Times New Roman, the red tone letters do not link properly. This is a test sequence; all these fonts support most IPA adequately.

Many typefaces have support for IPA characters, but good diacritic rendering remains rare.[[134]](#cite_note-Es_gilt_das_gesprochene_Wort:_Schriftarten_für_IPA-Transkriptionen-192) [Web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") generally do not need any configuration to display IPA characters, provided that a typeface capable of doing so is available to the operating system.

#### Free fonts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Free fonts")]

Typefaces that provide full IPA and nearly full extIPA support, including properly rendering the diacritics, include [Gentium](/wiki/Gentium "Gentium"), [Charis SIL](/wiki/Charis_SIL "Charis SIL"), [Doulos SIL](/wiki/Doulos_SIL "Doulos SIL"), and [Andika](/wiki/Andika_(font) "Andika (font)") developed by [SIL International](/wiki/SIL_International "SIL International"). Indeed, the IPA chose Doulos to publish their chart in Unicode format.
In addition to the level of support found in commercial and system fonts, these fonts support the full range of old-style (pre-Kiel) staveless tone letters, through a [character variant](/wiki/Character_variant "Character variant") option that suppresses the stave of the Chao tone letters. They also have an option to maintain the [a] ~ [ɑ] vowel distinction in italics. The only notable gaps are with the extIPA: the combining parentheses, which enclose diacritics, are not supported, nor is the enclosing 'cartouche' that is not included in Unicode.

The basic Latin [Noto fonts](/wiki/Noto_fonts "Noto fonts") commissioned by [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") also have significant IPA support, including diacritic placement, only failing with the more obscure IPA and extIPA characters and superscripts of the [Latin Extended-F](/wiki/Latin_Extended-F "Latin Extended-F") and [Latin Extended-G](/wiki/Latin_Extended-G "Latin Extended-G") blocks. The extIPA parentheses are included, but they do not enclose diacritics as they are supposed to.

[DejaVu](/wiki/DejaVu "DejaVu") is the second free Unicode font chosen by the IPA to publish their chart. It was last updated in 2016 and so does not support the Latin F or G blocks. The *sans* fonts are better than the *serif* ones, but even so stacked diacritics tend to overstrike each other.

#### Proprietary system fonts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Proprietary system fonts")]

[Calibri](/wiki/Calibri "Calibri"), the former default font of [Microsoft Office](/wiki/Microsoft_Office "Microsoft Office"), and [Times New Roman](/wiki/Times_New_Roman "Times New Roman") have nearly complete IPA support with good diacritic rendering, though they are not as complete as some free fonts (see image at right). Other widespread Microsoft fonts, such as [Arial](/wiki/Arial "Arial"), have poor support.

The Apple system fonts [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(typeface) "Geneva (typeface)"), [Lucida Grande](/wiki/Lucida_Grande "Lucida Grande") and [Hiragino](/wiki/Hiragino "Hiragino") (certain weights) have only basic IPA support.

#### Notable commercial fonts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Notable commercial fonts")]

[Brill](/wiki/Brill_Publishers#Brill_Typeface "Brill Publishers") has complete IPA and extIPA coverage of characters added to Unicode by 2020, with good diacritic and tone-letter support. It is a commercial font but is freely available for non-commercial use.[[135]](#cite_note-Brill_Typeface-193)

### ASCII and keyboard transliterations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: ASCII and keyboard transliterations")]

Further information: [Comparison of ASCII encodings of the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Comparison_of_ASCII_encodings_of_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Comparison of ASCII encodings of the International Phonetic Alphabet")

Several systems have been developed that map the IPA symbols to [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") characters. Notable systems include [SAMPA](/wiki/SAMPA "SAMPA") and [X-SAMPA](/wiki/X-SAMPA "X-SAMPA"). The usage of mapping systems in on-line text has to some extent been adopted in the context input methods, allowing convenient keying of IPA characters that would be otherwise unavailable on standard keyboard layouts.

### IETF language tags

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: IETF language tags")]

[IETF language tags](/wiki/IETF_language_tag "IETF language tag") have registered fonipa as a variant subtag identifying text as written in IPA.[[136]](#cite_note-IANA-194)
Thus, an IPA transcription of English could be tagged as en-fonipa.
For the use of IPA without attribution to a concrete language, und-fonipa is available.

### Computer input using on-screen keyboard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Computer input using on-screen keyboard")]

Online IPA keyboard utilities are available, though none of them cover the complete range of IPA symbols and diacritics. Examples are the *IPA 2018 i-charts* hosted by the IPA,[[137]](#cite_note-195) *IPA character picker* by Richard Ishida at GitHub,[[138]](#cite_note-196) *Type IPA phonetic symbols* at TypeIt.org,[[139]](#cite_note-197) and an *IPA Chart keyboard* by Weston Ruter also at GitHub.[[140]](#cite_note-198) In April 2019, Google's [Gboard](/wiki/Gboard "Gboard") for [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") added an IPA keyboard to its platform.[[141]](#cite_note-199)[[142]](#cite_note-200) For iOS there are multiple free keyboard layouts available, such as the *IPA Phonetic Keyboard*.[[143]](#cite_note-201)
