## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=International_Phonetic_Alphabet&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** The inverted bridge minus under the ⟨t̺ʰ⟩ specifies it as [apical](/wiki/Apical_consonant "Apical consonant") (pronounced with the tip of the tongue), and the superscript *h* shows that it is [aspirated](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant") (breathy); the rectangle under ⟨t̻⟩ specifies that it is [laminal](/wiki/Laminal "Laminal") (pronounced with the blade of the tongue). These details cause the English /t/ to sound different from the French /t/.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** "Originally, the aim was to make available a set of phonetic symbols which would be given *different* articulatory values, if necessary, in different languages."[[7]](#cite_note-8)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** "From its earliest days [...] the International Phonetic Association has aimed to provide 'a separate sign for each distinctive sound; that is, for each sound which, being used instead of another, in the same language, can change the meaning of a word' [...] what became widely known in the twentieth century as the phoneme."[[12]](#cite_note-International_Phonetic_Association_1999_27-14)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Exceptions are [affricates](/wiki/Affricate "Affricate") and [diphthongs](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong"), which may be written as either simple sequences, such as ⟨ts⟩ and ⟨au⟩, or with tie bars or diacritics such as ⟨t͜s⟩ and ⟨au̯⟩ to clarify that they are single sounds.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-17)** For instance, [flaps and taps](/wiki/Flap_consonant "Flap consonant") are two different kinds of [articulation](/wiki/Manner_of_articulation "Manner of articulation"), but since no language has (yet) been found to make a distinction between, say, an [alveolar flap](/wiki/Alveolar_flap "Alveolar flap") and an alveolar tap, the IPA does not provide such sounds with dedicated letters. Instead, it provides a single letter – in this case, [ɾ] – for both. Strictly speaking, this makes the IPA a partially [phonemic](/wiki/Phonemic "Phonemic") alphabet, not a purely [phonetic](/wiki/Phonetic "Phonetic") one.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** This exception to the rules was made primarily to explain why the IPA does not make a dental–alveolar distinction, despite one being phonemic in hundreds of languages, including most of the continent of Australia. [Americanist Phonetic Notation](/wiki/Americanist_Phonetic_Notation "Americanist Phonetic Notation") makes (or at least made) a distinction between apical ⟨t d s z n l⟩ and laminal ⟨τ δ ς ζ ν λ⟩, which is easily applicable to alveolar vs dental (when a language distinguishes apical alveolar from laminal dental, as in Australia), but despite several proposals to the Council, the IPA never voted to accept such a distinction.
   There are however other common phonemic distinctions that are made with diacritics, such as ⟨ʰ⟩ for aspirated consonants – a phonemic distinction made by thousands of languages – and ⟨ʲ⟩ for [palatalized](/wiki/Palatalization_(phonetics) "Palatalization (phonetics)") consonants, which were once transcribed by distinct letters in the IPA until those letters were retired in favor of the diacritic.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** There are three basic tone diacritics and five basic tone letters, both sets of which may be compounded.
8. **[^](#cite_ref-22)** "The non-roman letters of the International Phonetic Alphabet have been designed as far as possible to harmonize well with the roman letters. The Association does not recognize makeshift letters; It recognizes only letters which have been carefully cut so as to be in harmony with the other letters."[[14]](#cite_note-21)
9. **[^](#cite_ref-24)** "The new letters should be suggestive of the sounds they represent, by their resemblance to the old ones."[[15]](#cite_note-23)
10. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** Originally, [ʊ] was written as a small capital U. However, this was not easy to read, and so it was replaced with a turned small capital omega. In modern typefaces, it often has its own design, called a "horseshoe".
11. **[^](#cite_ref-27)** In addition, ⟨ɱ⟩ is an *ad hoc* derivation of ⟨m⟩ in imitation of ⟨ŋ⟩.
12. **[^](#cite_ref-46)** *[Pronunciation respelling for English](/wiki/Pronunciation_respelling_for_English "Pronunciation respelling for English")* contains detailed comparisons of respelling systems.
13. **[^](#cite_ref-47)** Monolingual Hebrew dictionaries use pronunciation respelling for words with unusual spelling; for example, the *[Even-Shoshan Dictionary](/wiki/Even-Shoshan_Dictionary "Even-Shoshan Dictionary")* respells תָּכְנִית‎ as ⟨תּוֹכְנִית‎⟩ because the word uses the *[kamatz](/wiki/Kamatz "Kamatz") katan*.
14. **[^](#cite_ref-48)** For example, [Sergey Ozhegov](/wiki/Sergey_Ozhegov "Sergey Ozhegov")'s dictionary adds [нэ́] in brackets to the French loan-word *пенсне* (**[pince-nez](/wiki/Pince-nez "Pince-nez")**) to indicate that the final ⟨[е](/wiki/%D0%95 "Е")⟩ does not [iotate](/wiki/Iotate "Iotate") the preceding ⟨[н](/wiki/%D0%9D "Н")⟩.
15. **[^](#cite_ref-50)** "In accordance with long-established Czech lexicographical tradition, a modified version of the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) is adopted in which letters of the Czech alphabet are employed."[[35]](#cite_note-49)
16. **[^](#cite_ref-58)** "Segments can usefully be divided into two major categories, consonants and vowels."[[42]](#cite_note-57)
17. **[^](#cite_ref-61)** They were moved "for presentational convenience [...] because of [their] rarity and the small number of types of sounds which are found there."[[44]](#cite_note-60)
18. **[^](#cite_ref-65)** "A symbol such as [β], shown on the chart in the position for a voiced bilabial fricative, can also be used to represent a voiced bilabial approximant if needed."[[47]](#cite_note-64)
19. **[^](#cite_ref-71)** It is traditional to place the tie bar above the letters. It may be placed below to avoid overlap with ascenders or diacritic marks, or simply because it is more legible that way, as in Niesler; Louw; Roux (2005). "Phonetic analysis of Afrikaans, English, Xhosa and Zulu using South African speech databases".[[52]](#cite_note-70)
20. **[^](#cite_ref-84)** Cf. the /ʷ.../ and /ʲ.../ transcriptions in Ernst-Kurdi, Eszter (2017). ["The Phonology of Mada"](https://www.sil.org/resources/archives/76424). SIL Yaoundé.
21. **[^](#cite_ref-85)** E.g. Dolgopolsky, Aaron (2013). [*Indo-European Dictionary with Nostratic Etymologies*](https://searchworks.stanford.edu/view/11657956). Studia Philologica. Rukopisnye pami͡atniki Drevneĭ Rusi.
22. **[^](#cite_ref-88)** The IPA *Handbook* variously defines the "linking" symbol as marking the "lack of a boundary"[[60]](#cite_note-86) or "absence of a break",[[61]](#cite_note-auto-87) and gives [French liaison](/wiki/French_liaison "French liaison") and English [linking r](/wiki/Linking_r "Linking r") as examples. It more generally means that the consonant ending one word forms a syllable with the vowel beginning the following word, across an orthographic space. However, the *Handbook* illustration for Croatian uses it to tie atonic [clitics](/wiki/Clitic "Clitic") to tonic words, with no resulting change in implied syllable structure.
23. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-global_90-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-global_90-1) The global rise and fall arrows come before the affected syllable or prosodic unit, like stress and upstep/downstep. This contrasts with the Chao tone letters (listed below), which most commonly come after. One will occasionally see a horizontal arrow ⟨→⟩ for global level pitch (only dropping due to [downdrift](/wiki/Downdrift "Downdrift")), e.g. in Julie Barbour (2012) *A Grammar of Neverver*. Additionally, some fonts display the arrows as [emoji](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji") by default, if the text-forcing [variation selector](/wiki/Variation_selector "Variation selector") ︎ is not appended.
24. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-pitch_92-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-pitch_92-1) There is not a one-to-one correspondence between tone/pitch diacritics and tone/pitch letters. When pitch is transcribed with diacritics, the three pitches ⟨é ē è⟩ are taken as the basic levels and are called 'high', 'mid' and 'low'. Contour tones in the *Handbook* combine only these three diacritics and are thus called ⟨e᷇⟩ 'high-mid' etc. More extreme pitches are ⟨e̋⟩ 'extra-high' and ⟨ȅ⟩ 'extra-low', using [doubling](#Comparative_degree) to indicate greater degree. These only rarely form contours in the literature, and the only compound diacritic available in Unicode (for 2026) is low to extra-high ⟨◌᫬⟩. When pitch is transcribed with the Chao tone letters, however, combinations of all five levels are readily accessible. Thus, ⟨e˥ e˧ e˩⟩ may be called 'high', 'mid' and 'low', with ⟨e˦ e˨⟩ being 'near-high' and 'near-low', analogous to descriptions of vowel height. In a three-level transcription, ⟨é ē è⟩ are identified with ⟨e˥ e˧ e˩⟩, but in a five-level transcription, ⟨e̋ ȅ⟩ are identified with ⟨e˥ e˩⟩.[[62]](#cite_note-91)
25. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-contour_93-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-contour_93-1) The peaking and dipping diacritics are uncommon and are not illustrated in the IPA *Handbook*. An example is /kla᷈/ 'properly' in [Mian](/wiki/Mian_language "Mian language").
26. **[^](#cite_ref-94)** Although any combination of tone diacritics is theoretically possible, such as ⟨e᪰⟩ for a falling–rising–falling tone, any others than those illustrated are rare. Double acute – grave is found for an extra-high falling tone in Valentin Vydrin, 2020, *Dan*. In Vossen & Dimmendaal (eds.) *The Oxford Handbook of African Languages*; and double acute – macron is found for an extrahigh-to-mid tone in Roger Blench, 2025, *Tone Systems in the Bantoid Languages*. Neither is supported by Unicode.
27. **[^](#cite_ref-97)** For example, ["Balearic"](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Balearic). *[Merriam-Webster.com Dictionary](/wiki/Merriam-Webster "Merriam-Webster")*. Merriam-Webster. [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [1032680871](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/1032680871).
28. **[^](#cite_ref-99)** Russian and Lithuanian sources and commonly use the character U+2E3D ⸽ VERTICAL SIX DOTS for a less-than-minor break, such as the slight break in list intonation (e.g. the very slight break between digits in a telephone number). U+2E3E ⸾ WIGGLY VERTICAL LINE is used for an unexpected interruption in or a sharp change of intonation.[[65]](#cite_note-98)
29. **[^](#cite_ref-103)** A work-around sometimes seen when a language has more than one rising or falling tone, and the author wishes to avoid the poorly legible diacritics ⟨e᷄, e᷅, e᷇, e᷆⟩ but does not wish to employ tone letters, is to restrict the generic rising ⟨ě⟩ and falling ⟨ê⟩ diacritics to the higher-pitched of the rising and falling tones, say /e˥˧/ and /e˧˥/, and to resurrect the retired (pre-Kiel) IPA subscript diacritics ⟨e̗⟩ and ⟨e̖⟩ for the lower-pitched rising and falling tones, say /e˩˧/ and /e˧˩/. When a language has either four or six level tones, the two middle tones are sometimes transcribed as high-mid ⟨e̍⟩ (non-standard) and low-mid ⟨ē⟩. Non-standard ⟨e̍⟩ is occasionally seen combined with acute and grave diacritics or with the macron to distinguish contour tones that involve the higher of the two mid tone levels; these are supported by Unicode in the [Combining Diacritical Marks Extended](/wiki/Combining_Diacritical_Marks_Extended "Combining Diacritical Marks Extended") block.
30. **[^](#cite_ref-108)** These include double acute–macron,[[69]](#cite_note-104) double acute–grave,[[70]](#cite_note-105)[[71]](#cite_note-mchugh-106) grave–double acute,[[71]](#cite_note-mchugh-106) double grave–acute and double grave–double acute.[[72]](#cite_note-107)
31. **[^](#cite_ref-110)** See for example [Pe Maung Tin](/wiki/Pe_Maung_Tin "Pe Maung Tin") (1924). "bɜˑmiːz". *Le Maître Phonétique*. 2 (39) (5): 4–5. [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [44704085](https://www.jstor.org/stable/44704085). where five pitch levels are distinguished.
32. **[^](#cite_ref-113)** The example has changed over the years. In the chart included in the 1999 IPA *Handbook*, it was [˦˥˦], and since the 2018 revision of the chart it has been [˧˦˨].
33. **[^](#cite_ref-114)** Chao did not include tone shapes such as [˨˦˦], [˧˩˩], which rise or fall and then level off (or vice versa). Such tone shapes are, however, frequently encountered in the modern literature.
34. **[^](#cite_ref-115)** In Chao's Sinological convention, a single tone letter ⟨˥⟩ is used for a high tone on a [checked syllable](/wiki/Checked_syllable "Checked syllable"), and a double tone letter ⟨˥˥⟩ for a high tone on an open syllable. Such redundant doubling is not used in the *Handbook*, where the tones of Cantonese [si˥] 'silk' and [sɪk˥] 'color' are transcribed the same way. If the author wishes to indicate a difference in phonetic or phonemic length, the IPA accomplishes that with the length marks ⟨◌̆ ◌ˑ ◌ː⟩ rather than through the tone letters.
35. **[^](#cite_ref-124)** This can also be indicated with superscript length marks for the aspiration, e.g. [pʰ], [tʰ𐞂], [kʰ𐞁], or with extIPA parentheses, e.g. [k⁽ʰ⁾], [kʰ]. Sometimes the obsolete transcription ⟨kʻ⟩ (with a turned apostrophe) for weak aspiration vs. ⟨kʰ⟩ for strong aspiration is still seen.
36. **[^](#cite_ref-128)** E.g. in [Laver 1994](#CITEREFLaver1994), pp. 559–560
37. **[^](#cite_ref-141)** For example, single and double pipe symbols are used for minor and major prosodic breaks. Although the *Handbook* specifies the prosodic symbols as being "thick" vertical lines, which would in theory be distinct from simple ASCII pipes used as delimiters (and similar to [Dania](/wiki/Dania_transcription "Dania transcription") transcription), this was an idea to keep them distinct from the otherwise similar pipes used as [click letters](/wiki/Click_letter "Click letter"), and is almost never found in practice.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoach198975-140) The *Handbook* assigns the prosodic pipe the Unicode encodings U+007C, which is the simple ASCII symbol, and the double pipe U+2016.[[61]](#cite_note-auto-87)
38. **[^](#cite_ref-MW_142-0)** For example, [Merriam-Webster](/wiki/Merriam-Webster "Merriam-Webster") dictionaries use [backslashes](/wiki/Backslash "Backslash") \ ... \ to demarcate their in-house diaphonemic transcription system. This contrasts with the *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")*, which uses / ... / to transcribe a specific target accent.
39. **[^](#cite_ref-143)** The proper angle brackets in Unicode are the mathematical symbols (U+27E8 and U+27E9). Chevrons ‹...› (U+2039, U+203A) are sometimes substituted, as in Americanist phonetic notation, as are the less-than and greater-than signs <...> (U+003C, U+003E) found on ASCII keyboards.
40. **[^](#cite_ref-158)** The motivation for this may vary. Some authors find the tie bars displeasing but the lack of tie bars confusing (i.e. ⟨č⟩ for /t͡ʃ/ as distinct from /tʃ/), while others simply prefer to have one letter for each segmental phoneme in a language.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
41. **[^](#cite_ref-160)** "At the 1989 Kiel Convention of the IPA, a sub-group was established to draw up recommendations for the transcription of disordered speech."[[113]](#cite_note-159)
42. **[^](#cite_ref-170)** As in [Afrasianist phonetic notation](/wiki/Afrasianist_phonetic_notation "Afrasianist phonetic notation"). ⟨S⟩ is particularly ambiguous. It has been used for 'stop', 'fricative', 'sibilant', 'sonorant' and 'semivowel'. On the other hand, plosive/stop is frequently abbreviated ⟨P⟩, ⟨S⟩ or (with non-tonal languages) \* ⟨T⟩. The illustrations given here use, as much as possible, letters that are capital versions of members of the sets they stand for: IPA [n] is a nasal and ⟨N⟩ is any nasal; [p] is a plosive, [f] a fricative, [s] a sibilant, [l] both a lateral and a liquid, [r] both a rhotic and a resonant, and [ʞ] a click. ⟨¢⟩ is an obstruent in Americanist notation, where it stands for [ts]. Alternative wildcards for 'glide', ⟨J⟩ and ⟨W⟩, partly fit this pattern, but are much less common than ⟨G⟩ in English-language sources.
43. **[^](#cite_ref-171)** In the context of ⟨CRV-⟩ syllables, the ⟨R⟩ is understood to include liquids and glides but to exclude nasals, as in Bennett (2020: 115) 'Click Phonology', in Sands (ed.), *Click Consonants*, Brill
44. **[^](#cite_ref-172)** {Close vowel} may instead be ⟨U⟩, and ⟨O⟩ may stand for {obstruent}.
45. **[^](#cite_ref-173)** Or glottal~pharyngeal ⟨H⟩, as in Afrasianist phonetic notation.
46. **[^](#cite_ref-174)** Somewhat more precisely, ⟨LM⟩ and ⟨MH⟩ are sometimes used for low and high rising tones, and ⟨HM⟩, ⟨ML⟩ for high and low falling tones; occasionally ⟨R⟩ for 'rising' or ⟨F⟩ for 'falling' is seen.
47. **[^](#cite_ref-175)** For other Turkic languages, ⟨I⟩ may be restricted to {ɯ i} (that is, to *ı i*), ⟨U⟩ to *u ü*, ⟨A⟩ to *a e* (or *a ä*), etc.
48. **[^](#cite_ref-178)** VoQS ⟨V⟩ does not mean phonetic voicing, nor a vowel; for example, in VoQS ⟨Ṽ⟩ is "nasal voice" (that is, a stretch of speech with added [nasalization](/wiki/Nasalization "Nasalization") in the speaker's voice), not a nasal vowel as it would be read in IPA notation.
49. **[^](#cite_ref-181)** "Diacritics may also be employed to create symbols for phonemes, thus reducing the need to create new letter shapes."[[12]](#cite_note-International_Phonetic_Association_1999_27-14)
50. **[^](#cite_ref-182)** Several dedicated letters have been proposed; see [Obsolete and nonstandard symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/Obsolete_and_nonstandard_symbols_in_the_International_Phonetic_Alphabet "Obsolete and nonstandard symbols in the International Phonetic Alphabet")
51. **[^](#cite_ref-185)** "The diacritics...can be used to modify the lip or tongue position implied by a vowel symbol."[[128]](#cite_note-184)
52. **[^](#cite_ref-191)** For example, the IPA *Handbook* lists ⟨p⟩ as "lower-case P" and ⟨χ⟩ as "chi."[[133]](#cite_note-190)
